title: How to stop languishing and start finding flow
en_source:
1
00:00:00,413 --> 00:00:02,707
I know you all have long to-do lists,

2
00:00:02,749 --> 00:00:07,503
but I hate wasting time so much that I have a to-don't list.

3
00:00:07,545 --> 00:00:09,547
Don't scroll on social media,

4
00:00:09,589 --> 00:00:11,257
don't check my phone in bed

5
00:00:11,299 --> 00:00:12,800
and don't turn on the TV

6
00:00:12,842 --> 00:00:15,678
unless I already know what I want to watch.

7
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:19,057
But last year I found myself breaking all of those rules.

8
00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:21,059
I was staying up way past midnight,

9
00:00:21,059 --> 00:00:22,351
doomscrolling,

10
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979
playing endless games of online Scrabble

11
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776
and bingeing entire seasons of TV shows that weren't even good.

12
00:00:30,777 --> 00:00:32,653
The next morning I'd wake up in a daze

13
00:00:32,653 --> 00:00:35,573
and swear, "Tonight in bed by 10:00."

14
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118
But it kept happening night after night for weeks.

15
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411
What was I thinking?

16
00:00:41,204 --> 00:00:42,830
As an organizational psychologist,

17
00:00:42,872 --> 00:00:45,625
I have spent my whole career studying motivation,

18
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463
so it really bothers me when I can't explain my own behavior.

19
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:55,593
I wasn't depressed. I still had hope. Wasn't burned out, had energy.

20
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429
Wasn't lonely, I was with my family.

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017
I just felt a little bit aimless and a little bit joyless.

22
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020
Eventually, I remembered there's a name for that feeling:

23
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396
languishing.

24
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234
Languishing as a sense of emptiness, stagnation and ennui.

25
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778
It was coined by a sociologist Corey Keyes

26
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989
and immortalized by a philosopher, Mariah Carey.

27
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:19,033
(Laughter)

28
00:01:19,033 --> 00:01:22,745
When you're languishing, it just feels like you're muddling through your days,

29
00:01:22,787 --> 00:01:25,164
looking at your life through a foggy windshield.

30
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:29,168
So I'm curious how many of you have felt like that over the past few months.

31
00:01:31,379 --> 00:01:34,507
OK, those of you who didn't have the energy to raise your hands --

32
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675
(Laughter)

33
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469
you might be languishing right now.

34
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722
And you over here who didn't laugh, you're definitely languishing.

35
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,350
Strangely enough --

36
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561
[How are you feeling today? Meh. Meh. Meh.]

37
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Laughter)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Some of you passed the quiz.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319
Strangely enough, what rescued me from that feeling was playing Mario Kart.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
But let's back up for a second.

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
In the early days of covid,

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
a lot of us were struggling with fear, grief and isolation.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
But as the pandemic dragged on with no end in sight,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
our acute anguish gave way to chronic languish.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
We were all living in “Groundhog Day.”

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
It felt like the whole world was stagnating.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
So I wrote an article to put languishing on the map.

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
I called it "the neglected middle child of mental health"

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
and I suggested it might be the dominant emotion of our time.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
And soon it was everywhere.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
I was seeing it all over the media,

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
being discussed by celebrities, by royalty.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
I've never seen people so excited

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
to talk about their utter lack of excitement.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Laughter)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
And --

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
I think --

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
I think that naming languishing

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
helped people make sense of some puzzling experiences.

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042
Why even after getting vaccinated

61
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462
people were having trouble looking forward to the rest of the year.

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
Why when "National Treasure" came on TV,

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
my wife already knew all the words by heart.

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
And why I was staying up way too late,

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475
falling victim to what's known as revenge bedtime procrastination.

66
00:03:07,516 --> 00:03:08,768
(Laughter)

67
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
We were looking for bliss in a blah day and purpose in a perpetual pandemic.

68
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
But languishing is not unique to a pandemic.

69
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,704
It's part of the human condition.

70
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166
Two decades of research show that languishing can disrupt your focus

71
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252
and dampen your motivation.

72
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,671
It's also a risk factor for depression

73
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
because languishing often lurks below the surface.

74
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
You might not notice when your drive is dwindling

75
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
or your delight is dulling

76
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
You’re indifferent to your own indifference,

77
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
which means you don't seek help

78
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
and you might not even do anything to help yourself.

79
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
Meh.

80
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
Languishing isn't just hard to spot, though.

81
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
In many cultures, it's hard to talk about, too.

82
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
When people ask, "How are you?,"

83
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
you're expected to say, "Great!"

84
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
or "Living my best life."

85
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
That's called toxic positivity.

86
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Laughter)

87
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
It's the pressure that we face to be optimistic and upbeat at all times.

88
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
If you say, "You know, I'm just OK,"

89
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
then people might encourage you to look on the bright side

90
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475
or count your blessings,

91
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935
which isn't just annoying.

92
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
It can actually be bad advice.

93
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
Can I get two volunteers?

94
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276
I will cold-call if I have to, don't all jump at once.

95
00:04:40,276 --> 00:04:41,902
OK, right over here.

96
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
You can come up to a mic

97
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
and can I get another volunteer right over there, up to this mic, please.

98
00:04:46,991 --> 00:04:49,035
A round of applause for our two volunteers.

99
00:04:49,076 --> 00:04:50,870
(Applause)

100
00:04:52,872 --> 00:04:54,373
Hi, what's your name?

101
00:04:54,415 --> 00:04:55,458
Martin: Martin.

102
00:04:55,458 --> 00:04:56,584
Adam Grant: Thank you.

103
00:04:56,584 --> 00:04:59,628
Can you tell us three good things about your life, please?

104
00:05:01,672 --> 00:05:06,177
Martin: I’m married and I’m healthy and I’m happy.

105
00:05:06,218 --> 00:05:08,679
AG: All right, I’m glad the marriage came in first.

106
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,848
Well done. OK, over here. What’s your name?

107
00:05:10,890 --> 00:05:11,891
Lee: Lee.

108
00:05:11,891 --> 00:05:14,727
AG: Lee, can you tell us 42 good things about your life?

109
00:05:16,270 --> 00:05:19,398
Lee: My cat Titchypoo, my dog Enzo.

110
00:05:19,398 --> 00:05:20,775
And so my wife, Jazz.

111
00:05:20,775 --> 00:05:22,568
AG: Third behind the dog and the cat.

112
00:05:22,568 --> 00:05:23,569
(Laughter)

113
00:05:23,611 --> 00:05:25,071
Well played.

114
00:05:25,071 --> 00:05:27,156
Lee: My children, Indio and Walter,

115
00:05:27,156 --> 00:05:28,866
Manchester United Football Club,

116
00:05:28,866 --> 00:05:30,659
my friends, TED.

117
00:05:31,702 --> 00:05:33,496
AG: TED coming in at ringing eighth.

118
00:05:33,496 --> 00:05:35,581
Lee: TED is very high, TED is very high.

119
00:05:35,623 --> 00:05:40,753
The poetry of C.S. Lewis, E.E. Cummings, Dylan Thomas.

120
00:05:40,795 --> 00:05:43,506
AG: You want to name all the poets you’ve ever heard of?

121
00:05:43,506 --> 00:05:45,341
Alright, Lee, thank you.

122
00:05:45,341 --> 00:05:46,842
We’re going to pause you there.

123
00:05:46,884 --> 00:05:48,886
Round of applause. Thank you both.

124
00:05:48,928 --> 00:05:50,638
(Applause)

125
00:05:52,473 --> 00:05:56,435
So for a long time, I assumed that people in Lee's position

126
00:05:56,435 --> 00:05:59,105
were going to be happier than Martin.

127
00:05:59,105 --> 00:06:02,483
But when I ran the experiment, I found the exact opposite.

128
00:06:02,483 --> 00:06:05,736
That people who are randomly assigned to count more blessings,

129
00:06:05,736 --> 00:06:08,280
are actually, on average, less happy

130
00:06:08,280 --> 00:06:11,242
because you start to run out of things to be optimistic about.

131
00:06:11,242 --> 00:06:13,244
And if you don't know that many poets ...

132
00:06:13,285 --> 00:06:14,995
(Laughter)

133
00:06:15,037 --> 00:06:18,249
The harder it is to find good things about your life,

134
00:06:18,290 --> 00:06:23,087
the more you feel like, well, maybe my life isn't that good.

135
00:06:23,129 --> 00:06:24,755
In the early days of the pandemic,

136
00:06:24,797 --> 00:06:29,635
researchers found that the best predictor of well-being was not optimism.

137
00:06:29,677 --> 00:06:30,928
It was flow.

138
00:06:31,971 --> 00:06:34,640
Flow is that feeling of being in the zone,

139
00:06:34,682 --> 00:06:37,268
coined by the psychologist Mihály Csíkszentmihályi.

140
00:06:38,144 --> 00:06:40,813
It's that state of total absorption in an activity.

141
00:06:40,813 --> 00:06:45,484
For you, it might be cooking or running or gardening

142
00:06:45,484 --> 00:06:46,986
where you lose track of time

143
00:06:46,986 --> 00:06:50,364
and you might even lose your sense of self.

144
00:06:50,364 --> 00:06:52,992
Flow is the appeal of a Netflix binge

145
00:06:52,992 --> 00:06:55,327
because you get transported into a different world

146
00:06:55,369 --> 00:06:57,163
and immersed in a story.

147
00:06:57,204 --> 00:07:02,084
But bingeing is a temporary escape from languishing, not a cure.

148
00:07:02,126 --> 00:07:05,921
At best, it leaves you with a bunch of asymmetric relationships.

149
00:07:05,963 --> 00:07:08,549
You might love hanging out with your friends:

150
00:07:08,591 --> 00:07:10,342
Chandler,

151
00:07:10,384 --> 00:07:11,635
Arya,

152
00:07:11,677 --> 00:07:12,970
Dwight,

153
00:07:13,012 --> 00:07:14,388
Buffy -- Buffy, anyone? --

154
00:07:14,430 --> 00:07:15,473
(Laughter)

155
00:07:15,514 --> 00:07:16,765
Joe Exotic,

156
00:07:17,766 --> 00:07:18,976
Peppa Pig.

157
00:07:19,643 --> 00:07:21,896
(Whispers) But they don't know you exist.

158
00:07:23,814 --> 00:07:27,693
Bingeing is passive engagement in a fictional world,

159
00:07:27,693 --> 00:07:30,571
peak flow depends on active participation

160
00:07:30,571 --> 00:07:32,323
in the real world,

161
00:07:32,323 --> 00:07:35,659
which is why I was so surprised to find my flow

162
00:07:35,701 --> 00:07:39,079
while driving a cartoon car in a Nintendo game.

163
00:07:41,040 --> 00:07:42,583
When the pandemic first started,

164
00:07:42,625 --> 00:07:45,711
all three of our kids were at home in online school,

165
00:07:45,753 --> 00:07:47,922
and that lasted for a full year.

166
00:07:47,963 --> 00:07:49,882
It was not easy.

167
00:07:49,924 --> 00:07:52,593
One day we found this on our six-year-old's report card.

168
00:07:52,676 --> 00:07:57,515
[can independently mute and unmute himself when requested to do so]

169
00:07:57,556 --> 00:07:59,183
(Laughter)

170
00:08:00,017 --> 00:08:03,979
You know, I know some adults who still haven't figured that out yet,

171
00:08:03,979 --> 00:08:06,815
not just online, but in real life, too.

172
00:08:06,815 --> 00:08:09,527
So I guess we had that to celebrate.

173
00:08:09,527 --> 00:08:13,781
But like many of you, we were isolated from extended family.

174
00:08:13,781 --> 00:08:16,367
My sister was halfway across the country.

175
00:08:16,367 --> 00:08:19,995
And one day we were reminiscing about how much we love playing Mario Kart

176
00:08:20,037 --> 00:08:21,330
as we were kids.

177
00:08:21,372 --> 00:08:24,458
And she said, "Well, we could all play together online now."

178
00:08:24,500 --> 00:08:26,752
Why don't we start a family game?

179
00:08:26,794 --> 00:08:28,587
And soon we were playing every day

180
00:08:28,629 --> 00:08:30,756
with a video call running at the same time.

181
00:08:31,632 --> 00:08:35,469
And after a couple of weeks I stopped feeling so blah.

182
00:08:35,511 --> 00:08:38,222
I was living zen in the art of Mario Kart.

183
00:08:38,264 --> 00:08:39,515
(Laughter)

184
00:08:39,557 --> 00:08:43,852
In the morning our kids were waking up, asking what time we would play.

185
00:08:43,852 --> 00:08:45,479
They were excited.

186
00:08:45,479 --> 00:08:48,774
And they loved it when I would gloat about an impending victory,

187
00:08:48,774 --> 00:08:51,527
only to be bombed by a flying blue shell

188
00:08:51,527 --> 00:08:54,154
and then just sit there watching all three of our kids

189
00:08:54,154 --> 00:08:57,074
drive past me to the finish line in tiny go-carts.

190
00:08:58,075 --> 00:09:01,412
We had so much fun that we started a new Saturday night tradition

191
00:09:01,453 --> 00:09:03,080
after the kids were asleep.

192
00:09:03,122 --> 00:09:04,415
Adult Mario Kart.

193
00:09:04,456 --> 00:09:05,666
(Laughter)

194
00:09:07,918 --> 00:09:09,962
So after reflecting on that experience,

195
00:09:10,004 --> 00:09:13,090
I'm proud to present to you for the first time

196
00:09:13,132 --> 00:09:15,926
my Mario Kart theory of peak flow.

197
00:09:17,094 --> 00:09:21,974
It has three conditions: mastery, mindfulness and mattering.

198
00:09:22,016 --> 00:09:23,726
Let's start with mastery.

199
00:09:23,726 --> 00:09:25,019
Mastery is something

200
00:09:25,019 --> 00:09:27,730
a lot of us have been having a hard time finding lately.

201
00:09:27,730 --> 00:09:28,897
(Laughter)

202
00:09:28,897 --> 00:09:31,191
Psychologists find that at work

203
00:09:31,191 --> 00:09:34,278
the strongest factor in daily motivation and joy

204
00:09:34,278 --> 00:09:36,030
is a sense of progress.

205
00:09:36,947 --> 00:09:40,492
We find that our happiness depends in Western cultures

206
00:09:40,492 --> 00:09:44,747
more on how our projects are going today than how they went yesterday.

207
00:09:44,788 --> 00:09:46,874
That's why Nike says, "Just do it."

208
00:09:47,958 --> 00:09:52,212
I guess if Nike had been started in a more past-focused country like China,

209
00:09:52,254 --> 00:09:54,506
their slogan would be, "Just did it."

210
00:09:57,176 --> 00:10:01,722
If languishing is stagnation, flow involves momentum.

211
00:10:01,764 --> 00:10:05,017
But mastery does not have to be a big accomplishment,

212
00:10:05,059 --> 00:10:07,102
it can be small wins.

213
00:10:07,102 --> 00:10:10,356
Small wins explain why I was drawn to online Scrabble

214
00:10:10,356 --> 00:10:13,525
for the rush of playing a seven-letter word.

215
00:10:13,525 --> 00:10:16,362
Small wins makes sense of why so many people were thrilled

216
00:10:16,362 --> 00:10:18,947
to bake their first loaf of sourdough bread.

217
00:10:18,947 --> 00:10:23,577
And small wins explain why one engineer spent an entire afternoon

218
00:10:23,619 --> 00:10:28,374
mastering the art of stacking M&M's on top of each other.

219
00:10:28,415 --> 00:10:29,667
Take a look.

220
00:10:30,334 --> 00:10:32,711
(Video) This is going to be harder than I thought.

221
00:10:39,468 --> 00:10:40,761
Oh!

222
00:10:41,261 --> 00:10:42,721
Oh!

223
00:10:42,763 --> 00:10:44,598
Five M&Ms! Five M&Ms!

224
00:10:46,141 --> 00:10:47,351
(Laughter)

225
00:10:48,560 --> 00:10:50,562
AG: Turns out that was a world record.

226
00:10:50,562 --> 00:10:52,147
(Laughter)

227
00:10:52,147 --> 00:10:55,818
That kind of mastery depends on a second condition for flow,

228
00:10:55,818 --> 00:10:57,111
mindfulness.

229
00:10:57,111 --> 00:11:00,447
Focusing your full attention on a single task,

230
00:11:00,447 --> 00:11:04,201
not something a lot of us are doing that much these days.

231
00:11:04,243 --> 00:11:07,913
[Are you OK? You’re barely paying attention to your book, phone, show ... ]

232
00:11:07,913 --> 00:11:10,791
[ ... laptop and the crossword you started ten minutes ago.]

233
00:11:10,833 --> 00:11:15,879
There's evidence that on average, people are checking emails 74 times a day,

234
00:11:15,921 --> 00:11:20,008
switching tasks every 10 minutes,

235
00:11:20,050 --> 00:11:23,011
and that creates what's been called time confetti,

236
00:11:23,053 --> 00:11:25,848
where we take what could be meaningful moments of our lives

237
00:11:25,889 --> 00:11:27,141
and we shred them

238
00:11:27,182 --> 00:11:29,560
into increasingly tiny, useless pieces.

239
00:11:30,769 --> 00:11:34,982
Time confetti is an enemy of both energy and of excellence.

240
00:11:34,982 --> 00:11:37,985
If we want to find flow, we need better boundaries.

241
00:11:38,068 --> 00:11:42,448
[It keeps me from looking at my phone every two seconds.]

242
00:11:42,614 --> 00:11:43,907
(Laughter)

243
00:11:45,492 --> 00:11:46,952
When I think about boundaries,

244
00:11:46,994 --> 00:11:50,706
I think of an experiment by organizational scholar Leslie Perlow.

245
00:11:50,748 --> 00:11:55,127
She went to a Fortune 500 company and she tested a quiet time policy.

246
00:11:55,169 --> 00:11:58,505
No interruptions three mornings a week before noon.

247
00:11:59,423 --> 00:12:02,384
On average, engineers spiked in productivity.

248
00:12:02,426 --> 00:12:05,596
47 percent of them were more productive than usual.

249
00:12:05,637 --> 00:12:09,933
But the best part is that when the company made quiet time official policy,

250
00:12:09,933 --> 00:12:12,811
they had 65 percent above average productivity.

251
00:12:13,729 --> 00:12:15,606
I don't think there's anything magical

252
00:12:15,606 --> 00:12:18,192
about Tuesday, Thursday, Friday before noon.

253
00:12:18,192 --> 00:12:21,612
The lesson here is that we need to treat uninterrupted blocks of time

254
00:12:21,612 --> 00:12:23,280
as treasures to guard.

255
00:12:24,698 --> 00:12:27,993
Now, mastery and mindfulness will get you to flow,

256
00:12:28,035 --> 00:12:31,413
but there's a third condition that turns it into a peak experience.

257
00:12:31,455 --> 00:12:35,375
Mattering. Knowing that you make a difference to other people.

258
00:12:36,293 --> 00:12:38,879
Early in my career, I was studying fundraising callers

259
00:12:38,921 --> 00:12:42,090
who were trying to bring in alumni donations to a university,

260
00:12:42,132 --> 00:12:46,303
and I knew they were languishing when I saw this sign posted on their wall.

261
00:12:46,345 --> 00:12:50,349
[Doing a good job here is like wetting your pants in a dark suit]

262
00:12:50,390 --> 00:12:52,684
[You get a warm feeling but no one else notices]

263
00:12:52,726 --> 00:12:54,311
(Laughter)

264
00:12:54,478 --> 00:12:57,731
I wanted to study how to show them that their work mattered.

265
00:12:57,731 --> 00:13:00,108
So I designed a series of experiments

266
00:13:00,108 --> 00:13:03,153
and over the next month, one group of callers on average

267
00:13:03,153 --> 00:13:05,614
more than doubled in weekly time on the phone

268
00:13:05,614 --> 00:13:08,826
and nearly tripled in weekly revenue.

269
00:13:08,826 --> 00:13:12,788
What moved the needle was randomly assigning them to meet one student

270
00:13:12,830 --> 00:13:15,207
whose scholarship had been funded by their work.

271
00:13:16,124 --> 00:13:19,628
Now, instead of focusing on the monotonous process of making calls,

272
00:13:19,670 --> 00:13:23,924
they were absorbed in a meaningful purpose of helping to fund tuition.

273
00:13:24,925 --> 00:13:28,929
So think about the people who would be worse off if your job didn't exist.

274
00:13:28,971 --> 00:13:31,557
Those are the people who make your work matter.

275
00:13:31,598 --> 00:13:35,060
You need to know their names, their faces and their stories,

276
00:13:35,060 --> 00:13:37,813
and you can find flow in projects that benefit them.

277
00:13:39,106 --> 00:13:42,818
This all explains why Mario Kart was such a great experience for me.

278
00:13:42,818 --> 00:13:44,736
It gave me a feeling of mastery,

279
00:13:44,736 --> 00:13:48,532
the sweet satisfaction of a perfectly placed banana peel

280
00:13:48,532 --> 00:13:50,576
for my sister to slip on.

281
00:13:51,910 --> 00:13:53,620
It required mindfulness too.

282
00:13:53,662 --> 00:13:55,539
My brother-in-law was the best player.

283
00:13:55,581 --> 00:13:57,791
Beating him demanded total concentration,

284
00:13:57,833 --> 00:14:00,711
especially when my kids were ganging up with him against me.

285
00:14:01,837 --> 00:14:04,590
And it wasn't just a game. It mattered.

286
00:14:05,465 --> 00:14:09,720
Over the past year, we've all felt helpless in one way or another.

287
00:14:09,761 --> 00:14:11,638
I felt helpless to fix covid.

288
00:14:11,680 --> 00:14:14,516
I couldn't even do that much to make online school better.

289
00:14:14,558 --> 00:14:15,767
And I'm a teacher.

290
00:14:16,476 --> 00:14:19,855
But in Mario Kart, I felt helpful.

291
00:14:19,855 --> 00:14:22,524
I was able to give my kids something to look forward to

292
00:14:22,524 --> 00:14:24,026
when we couldn't go anywhere.

293
00:14:24,026 --> 00:14:27,237
I was able to keep my family close when we were far apart.

294
00:14:28,530 --> 00:14:31,116
We normally think of flow as an individual experience.

295
00:14:31,116 --> 00:14:34,411
But playing Nintendo, we were all immersed together.

296
00:14:34,453 --> 00:14:36,496
And although we don't play daily anymore,

297
00:14:36,538 --> 00:14:38,999
I feel closer to my sister and my brother-in-law

298
00:14:39,041 --> 00:14:41,043
than I ever had before.

299
00:14:41,084 --> 00:14:44,755
I learned that love is not the frequency of communication,

300
00:14:44,796 --> 00:14:46,298
it's the depth of connections.

301
00:14:47,674 --> 00:14:50,552
I also realized that the antidote to languishing

302
00:14:50,594 --> 00:14:53,013
does not have to be something productive,

303
00:14:53,055 --> 00:14:54,932
it can be something joyful.

304
00:14:55,766 --> 00:15:00,604
Our peak moments of flow are having fun with the people we love,

305
00:15:00,604 --> 00:15:04,024
which is now a daily task on my to-do list.

306
00:15:04,983 --> 00:15:06,985
So what's your version of Mario Kart?

307
00:15:06,985 --> 00:15:10,697
Where do you find mastery and mindfulness with the people who matter to you?

308
00:15:11,782 --> 00:15:13,951
I think we need to rethink our understanding

309
00:15:13,951 --> 00:15:15,827
of mental health and well-being.

310
00:15:15,869 --> 00:15:20,040
Not depressed doesn't mean you're not struggling.

311
00:15:20,082 --> 00:15:21,667
Not burned out

312
00:15:21,708 --> 00:15:23,877
doesn't mean you're fired up.

313
00:15:23,919 --> 00:15:26,254
When someone says, "How are you?,"

314
00:15:26,296 --> 00:15:29,883
it's OK to say, "Honestly, I'm languishing."

315
00:15:30,884 --> 00:15:33,720
Or if you can only muster one syllable,

316
00:15:33,762 --> 00:15:35,013
"Meh."

317
00:15:35,055 --> 00:15:36,306
(Laughter)

318
00:15:36,348 --> 00:15:39,309
And when you're ready, you can start finding the flow

319
00:15:39,309 --> 00:15:41,812
that lights a path out of the void.

320
00:15:41,812 --> 00:15:42,854
Thank you.

321
00:15:42,896 --> 00:15:46,191
(Applause)

Full English transcript to translate (translate ALL cues, do not stop until the last one):

1
00:00:00,413 --> 00:00:02,707
I know you all have long to-do lists,

2
00:00:02,749 --> 00:00:07,503
but I hate wasting time so much that I have a to-don't list.

3
00:00:07,545 --> 00:00:09,547
Don't scroll on social media,

4
00:00:09,589 --> 00:00:11,257
don't check my phone in bed

5
00:00:11,299 --> 00:00:12,800
and don't turn on the TV

6
00:00:12,842 --> 00:00:15,678
unless I already know what I want to watch.

7
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:19,057
But last year I found myself breaking all of those rules.

8
00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:21,059
I was staying up way past midnight,

9
00:00:21,059 --> 00:00:22,351
doomscrolling,

10
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979
playing endless games of online Scrabble

11
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776
and bingeing entire seasons of TV shows that weren't even good.

12
00:00:30,777 --> 00:00:32,653
The next morning I'd wake up in a daze

13
00:00:32,653 --> 00:00:35,573
and swear, "Tonight in bed by 10:00."

14
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118
But it kept happening night after night for weeks.

15
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411
What was I thinking?

16
00:00:41,204 --> 00:00:42,830
As an organizational psychologist,

17
00:00:42,872 --> 00:00:45,625
I have spent my whole career studying motivation,

18
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463
so it really bothers me when I can't explain my own behavior.

19
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:55,593
I wasn't depressed. I still had hope. Wasn't burned out, had energy.

20
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429
Wasn't lonely, I was with my family.

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017
I just felt a little bit aimless and a little bit joyless.

22
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020
Eventually, I remembered there's a name for that feeling:

23
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396
languishing.

24
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234
Languishing as a sense of emptiness, stagnation and ennui.

25
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778
It was coined by a sociologist Corey Keyes

26
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989
and immortalized by a philosopher, Mariah Carey.

27
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:19,033
(Laughter)

28
00:01:19,033 --> 00:01:22,745
When you're languishing, it just feels like you're muddling through your days,

29
00:01:22,787 --> 00:01:25,164
looking at your life through a foggy windshield.

30
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:29,168
So I'm curious how many of you have felt like that over the past few months.

31
00:01:31,379 --> 00:01:34,507
OK, those of you who didn't have the energy to raise your hands --

32
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675
(Laughter)

33
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469
you might be languishing right now.

34
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722
And you over here who didn't laugh, you're definitely languishing.

35
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,350
Strangely enough --

36
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561
[How are you feeling today? Meh. Meh. Meh.]

37
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603
(Laughter)

38
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021
Some of you passed the quiz.

39
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319
Strangely enough, what rescued me from that feeling was playing Mario Kart.

40
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530
But let's back up for a second.

41
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740
In the early days of covid,

42
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911
a lot of us were struggling with fear, grief and isolation.

43
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873
But as the pandemic dragged on with no end in sight,

44
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209
our acute anguish gave way to chronic languish.

45
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170
We were all living in “Groundhog Day.”

46
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964
It felt like the whole world was stagnating.

47
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509
So I wrote an article to put languishing on the map.

48
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054
I called it "the neglected middle child of mental health"

49
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,892
and I suggested it might be the dominant emotion of our time.

50
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852
And soon it was everywhere.

51
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:29,604
I was seeing it all over the media,

52
00:02:29,604 --> 00:02:32,356
being discussed by celebrities, by royalty.

53
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650
I've never seen people so excited

54
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278
to talk about their utter lack of excitement.

55
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198
(Laughter)

56
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449
And --

57
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910
I think --

58
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870
I think that naming languishing

59
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207
helped people make sense of some puzzling experiences.

60
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042
Why even after getting vaccinated

61
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462
people were having trouble looking forward to the rest of the year.

62
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,173
Why when "National Treasure" came on TV,

63
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468
my wife already knew all the words by heart.

64
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471
And why I was staying up way too late,

65
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475
falling victim to what's known as revenge bedtime procrastination.

66
00:03:07,516 --> 00:03:08,768
(Laughter)

67
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949
We were looking for bliss in a blah day and purpose in a perpetual pandemic.

68
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:26,619
But languishing is not unique to a pandemic.

69
00:03:26,661 --> 00:03:28,704
It's part of the human condition.

70
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166
Two decades of research show that languishing can disrupt your focus

71
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252
and dampen your motivation.

72
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,671
It's also a risk factor for depression

73
00:03:36,712 --> 00:03:39,840
because languishing often lurks below the surface.

74
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,426
You might not notice when your drive is dwindling

75
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595
or your delight is dulling

76
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598
You’re indifferent to your own indifference,

77
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433
which means you don't seek help

78
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646
and you might not even do anything to help yourself.

79
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897
Meh.

80
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235
Languishing isn't just hard to spot, though.

81
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571
In many cultures, it's hard to talk about, too.

82
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448
When people ask, "How are you?,"

83
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659
you're expected to say, "Great!"

84
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,036
or "Living my best life."

85
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123
That's called toxic positivity.

86
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292
(Laughter)

87
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217
It's the pressure that we face to be optimistic and upbeat at all times.

88
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,803
If you say, "You know, I'm just OK,"

89
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098
then people might encourage you to look on the bright side

90
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475
or count your blessings,

91
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935
which isn't just annoying.

92
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021
It can actually be bad advice.

93
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:36,772
Can I get two volunteers?

94
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276
I will cold-call if I have to, don't all jump at once.

95
00:04:40,276 --> 00:04:41,902
OK, right over here.

96
00:04:41,902 --> 00:04:43,195
You can come up to a mic

97
00:04:43,195 --> 00:04:46,991
and can I get another volunteer right over there, up to this mic, please.

98
00:04:46,991 --> 00:04:49,035
A round of applause for our two volunteers.

99
00:04:49,076 --> 00:04:50,870
(Applause)

100
00:04:52,872 --> 00:04:54,373
Hi, what's your name?

00:00:19,098 --> 00:00:21,059  
我凌晨过后才睡觉，  

9  
00:00:21,059 --> 00:00:22,351  
持续刷手机，  

10  
00:00:22,351 --> 00:00:24,979  
在线玩无止境的拼字游戏，  

11  
00:00:24,979 --> 00:00:29,776  
还追整季质量平平的电视剧。  

12  
00:00:30,777 --> 00:00:32,653  
第二天早晨我昏昏沉沉地醒来，  

13  
00:00:32,653 --> 00:00:35,573  
发誓“今晚必须10点上床”。  

14  
00:00:35,573 --> 00:00:39,118  
但这重复了整整几周。  

15  
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:40,411  
我在想什么？  

16  
00:00:41,204 --> 00:00:42,830  
作为一名组织心理学家，  

17  
00:00:42,872 --> 00:00:45,625  
我半辈子研究动机这个课题，  

18  
00:00:45,666 --> 00:00:50,463  
但对自己行为的解释却漏洞百出，这让我特别困扰。  

19  
00:00:50,505 --> 00:00:55,593  
当时我并不抑郁，还有希望；不疲惫，有精力；不孤单，与家人同在。  

20  
00:00:55,635 --> 00:00:58,429  
我只是觉得方向感模糊、兴致全无。  

21  
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:03,017  
后来我终于想起这情绪有名字——  

22  
00:01:03,017 --> 00:01:06,020  
“虚耗”。  

23  
00:01:06,020 --> 00:01:07,396  
虚空、停滞与倦怠感。  

24  
00:01:07,396 --> 00:01:11,234  
社会学家科尔·凯兹创造了这个词，哲学家玛丽亚·凯莉让它永存。  

25  
00:01:11,234 --> 00:01:13,778  
（笑声）  

26  
00:01:13,778 --> 00:01:16,989  
当虚耗时，就像在雾蒙蒙的车窗后度日，  

27  
00:01:16,989 --> 00:01:25,206  
有人知道过去几个月做过类似的“糊弄生活”吗？  

28  
00:01:25,206 --> 00:01:34,549  
那些懒得举手的人——  

29  
00:01:34,549 --> 00:01:35,675  
（笑声）  

30  
00:01:35,716 --> 00:01:38,469  
可能就是当下正在虚耗的人。  

31  
00:01:38,511 --> 00:01:41,722  
那边没笑的同胞们——你们正深陷其中。  

32  
00:01:43,099 --> 00:01:44,350  
奇怪的是——  

33  
00:01:44,350 --> 00:01:46,561  
{今天心情怎么样？无聊。无聊。无聊。}  

34  
00:01:46,602 --> 00:01:47,603  
（笑声）  

35  
00:01:47,603 --> 00:01:49,021  
有人通过了本次测试。  

36  
00:01:49,939 --> 00:01:55,319  
更奇特的是，让我走出虚耗的竟是玩《马里奥赛车》这类游戏。  

37  
00:01:56,028 --> 00:01:57,530  
不过先倒带回想疫情初期——  

38  
00:01:58,322 --> 00:01:59,740  
最初几个月，我们饱受恐惧、哀伤和隔离之苦，  

39  
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:03,911  
但随着疫情持续、终局未明，  

40  
00:02:03,953 --> 00:02:06,873  
我们的急性痛苦转为慢性虚耗——  

41  
00:02:06,914 --> 00:02:10,209  
仿佛全人类都陷入《土拨鼠之日》的循环。  

42  
00:02:10,251 --> 00:02:12,170  
世界停滞不前。  

43  
00:02:12,211 --> 00:02:14,964  
于是我写篇文章，让虚耗概念为人所知—-  

44  
00:02:15,006 --> 00:02:18,509  
将它称作“心理健康被忽视的中间儿童”  

45  
00:02:18,551 --> 00:02:22,054  
并推测这可能是我们时代的主导情绪。  

46  
00:02:22,096 --> 00:02:25,893  
没想到这个词突然四处蔓延。  

47  
00:02:25,933 --> 00:02:27,852  
媒体、明星、王室都在讨论它，  

48  
00:02:27,852 --> 00:02:32,356  
人们前所未有地热衷谈论自身的兴致缺缺。  

49  
00:02:32,356 --> 00:02:34,650  
（笑声）  

50  
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:37,278  
或许命名“虚耗”  

51  
00:02:37,278 --> 00:02:40,198  
让人们找到了混乱的注解。  

52  
00:02:40,198 --> 00:02:41,449  
比如——  

53  
00:02:42,366 --> 00:02:43,910  
我想——  

54  
00:02:43,951 --> 00:02:45,870  
为什么接种疫苗后仍难期待未来，  

55  
00:02:45,912 --> 00:02:49,207  
为什么《国家宝藏》上映时我太太已背下所有歌词，  

56  
00:02:49,248 --> 00:02:51,042  
为什么熬夜搞起“报复性睡前拖延症”——  

57  
00:02:51,083 --> 00:02:54,462  
即使经历疫苗危机，  

58  
00:02:54,503 --> 00:02:57,471  
我们仍在乏味日子里搜寻快乐，在疫情泥沼中寻找意义。  

59  
00:02:57,215 --> 00:03:00,468  
（笑声）  

60  
00:03:00,509 --> 00:03:03,471  
虚耗并非疫情专属，  

61  
00:03:03,512 --> 00:03:07,475  
而是人类境遇的一部分。  

62  
00:03:07,516 --> 00:03:08,768  
二十年的研究表明——  

63  
00:03:17,693 --> 00:03:22,949  
虚耗会干扰专注力、消磨干劲，  

64  
00:03:23,908 --> 00:03:28,746  
更是抑郁症的潜在诱因。  

65  
00:03:28,746 --> 00:03:32,166  
你可能没察觉动力消退、乐趣流失——  

66  
00:03:32,208 --> 00:03:34,252  
对无动于衷的无动于衷，  

67  
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:39,840  
使你既不愿求助，也不主动自救。  

68  
00:03:39,882 --> 00:03:42,468  
你觉察不到自己正在失去。  

69  
00:03:42,468 --> 00:03:44,595  
（停顿）  

70  
00:03:44,637 --> 00:03:47,598  
（停顿）  

71  
00:03:47,640 --> 00:03:49,433  
Meh.  

72  
00:03:49,433 --> 00:03:53,646  
虚耗不仅难以察觉，  

73  
00:03:53,646 --> 00:03:54,897  
在许多文化里，它也难于开口。  

74  
00:03:56,065 --> 00:03:59,235  
问“最近如何？”时，人们总是回答“很棒！”  

75  
00:03:59,235 --> 00:04:02,571  
或“正在创造人生巅峰”——  

76  
00:04:02,571 --> 00:04:04,448  
这就叫“有毒的正能量”。  

77  
00:04:04,448 --> 00:04:06,659  
（笑声）  

78  
00:04:06,659 --> 00:04:09,954  
若你说“我就是一般般”，  

79  
00:04:09,954 --> 00:04:12,123  
别人就会施加“积极压力”。  

80  
00:04:12,164 --> 00:04:14,292  
若说“要多知足呢”，  

81  
00:04:16,919 --> 00:04:23,217  
这不仅是冒犯，甚至有害。  

82  
00:04:23,259 --> 00:04:25,845  
现在需要两位志愿者！  

83  
00:04:25,845 --> 00:04:29,098  
我可以当人肉呼叫器——请别一拥而上。  

84  
00:04:29,140 --> 00:04:31,475  
这位朋友，请到这边话筒前——  

85  
00:04:31,475 --> 00:04:32,935  
那边也请一位到另一台话筒。  

86  
00:04:32,935 --> 00:04:35,021  
给我们两位志愿者一些掌声！  

87  
00:04:35,021 --> 00:04:37,690  
（掌声）  

88  
00:04:37,690 --> 00:04:40,276  
你好，叫什么名字？

title: The link between menopause and gender inequity at work
en_source:
1
00:00:01,873 --> 00:00:07,045
Of America's 500 largest companies, only 42 have female CEOs.

2
00:00:07,086 --> 00:00:08,796
And if you look at other countries,

3
00:00:08,838 --> 00:00:11,424
the data is similar, and in some cases, worse.

4
00:00:11,924 --> 00:00:14,052
And, of course, there are a number of factors

5
00:00:14,052 --> 00:00:16,929
contributing to global gender inequity in the workplace.

6
00:00:16,971 --> 00:00:20,683
But I believe that there is one factor often underecognized,

7
00:00:20,725 --> 00:00:22,268
and that's menopause.

8
00:00:22,894 --> 00:00:24,479
So what do I mean?

9
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:27,315
Many women will rise into senior positions of leadership

10
00:00:27,356 --> 00:00:29,192
in their 40s or their 50s.

11
00:00:29,192 --> 00:00:31,736
The average age of a CEO is mid-50s.

12
00:00:32,153 --> 00:00:35,364
Perimenopause, or the transition into menopause,

13
00:00:35,406 --> 00:00:39,535
generally occurs also between 45 and 55.

14
00:00:39,535 --> 00:00:41,621
And this is where the symptoms really start,

15
00:00:41,621 --> 00:00:43,498
and it can last up to 10 years.

16
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335
So just as a woman is stepping up, taking the reins,

17
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,170
earning at her highest potential,

18
00:00:49,212 --> 00:00:51,297
her body can feel like it's betraying her.

19
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:55,134
Now, for anyone who doesn't know, which turns out to be many of us --

20
00:00:55,176 --> 00:00:58,471
for me, I had my first conversation with my own mother about menopause

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:00,056
in the last few years --

22
00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,724
it's a big deal.

23
00:01:01,724 --> 00:01:04,685
And it's not just a big deal for women over 45.

24
00:01:04,685 --> 00:01:07,605
Nonbinary, transmasculine and younger women

25
00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233
may all cope with menopausal symptoms.

26
00:01:10,775 --> 00:01:12,401
So what are these symptoms?

27
00:01:13,027 --> 00:01:14,237
They can be physical,

28
00:01:14,237 --> 00:01:20,201
so that's hot flushes, joint pain, urinary incontinence, heavy periods.

29
00:01:20,201 --> 00:01:23,746
They can also be mental: anxiety, depression,

30
00:01:23,788 --> 00:01:26,916
bouts of low confidence, difficulty sleeping.

31
00:01:26,958 --> 00:01:30,253
Imagine that -- 10 years of difficulty sleeping.

32
00:01:31,129 --> 00:01:34,257
The list is long and symptoms can change,

33
00:01:34,298 --> 00:01:36,592
so it means you never really know what to expect.

34
00:01:37,051 --> 00:01:39,554
But make no mistake: they are significant.

35
00:01:39,554 --> 00:01:44,058
So significant, that in a study by Vodafone across five countries,

36
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,062
it found that 60 percent of women dealing with menopausal symptoms

37
00:01:48,062 --> 00:01:50,314
said it impacted their work.

38
00:01:50,356 --> 00:01:52,608
In another study in the UK,

39
00:01:52,650 --> 00:01:57,238
30 percent of women said they missed work due to their symptoms.

40
00:01:57,280 --> 00:02:01,450
And at the extremes, women are making serious choices about their careers.

41
00:02:01,450 --> 00:02:04,871
As many as 11 percent forewent a promotion opportunity,

42
00:02:04,912 --> 00:02:08,207
and as many as eight percent resigned from their positions

43
00:02:08,249 --> 00:02:10,126
because of menopausal symptoms.

44
00:02:10,585 --> 00:02:14,380
My fascination with female leadership and the obvious gap

45
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:18,259
began as a strategy consultant working in boardrooms around the world.

46
00:02:18,301 --> 00:02:20,928
In over 10 years, I can count on one hand

47
00:02:20,970 --> 00:02:25,975
the number of senior women over 50 in executive positions that I saw,

48
00:02:26,017 --> 00:02:29,770
whether it was Seattle or Dubai, Lagos or upstate New York,

49
00:02:29,812 --> 00:02:31,397
women were scarce at the top.

50
00:02:32,565 --> 00:02:35,860
I started to look around and question the system I was in,

51
00:02:35,902 --> 00:02:38,529
wonder what opportunities really existed.

52
00:02:38,571 --> 00:02:41,199
The women at the top were exceptional.

53
00:02:41,199 --> 00:02:43,492
The bar seemed almost too high to reach.

54
00:02:43,993 --> 00:02:48,456
And so I started speaking to my friends and colleagues in their 40s and 50s.

55
00:02:48,456 --> 00:02:52,793
They talked about things I knew: balancing a serious job, teenagers

56
00:02:52,835 --> 00:02:55,004
and caring for elderly parents.

57
00:02:55,004 --> 00:02:58,382
But in a few instances, close friends opened up.

58
00:02:58,424 --> 00:03:01,552
They talked about how their bodies and their minds were changing

59
00:03:01,552 --> 00:03:05,097
and the impact of menopause on many aspects of their lives,

60
00:03:05,139 --> 00:03:06,349
including work.

61
00:03:07,391 --> 00:03:09,435
It was at that point that I realized

62
00:03:09,477 --> 00:03:12,188
if I wanted to make a difference to women in the workplace

63
00:03:12,230 --> 00:03:15,274
so we can all work as long as we want to,

64
00:03:15,274 --> 00:03:17,151
it was to improve menopause care.

65
00:03:18,110 --> 00:03:20,613
I believe the workplace can play a major role

66
00:03:20,655 --> 00:03:24,533
in improving the experience for women coping with menopausal symptoms.

67
00:03:24,533 --> 00:03:28,120
And it might sound cliché, but it does start with awareness.

68
00:03:28,162 --> 00:03:31,791
And then it means changing the physical setup of our offices,

69
00:03:31,832 --> 00:03:34,293
resetting expectations around work culture

70
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,254
and updating health care policies.

71
00:03:36,671 --> 00:03:38,756
So, first: awareness.

72
00:03:38,798 --> 00:03:42,593
Since we learn virtually nothing about menopause in school,

73
00:03:42,593 --> 00:03:44,762
in the news, in pop culture,

74
00:03:44,804 --> 00:03:48,849
it's impossible to expect that even the most well-intentioned manager

75
00:03:48,891 --> 00:03:51,143
would have a clue how to be supportive.

76
00:03:51,644 --> 00:03:57,358
So many societies place so much pressure on women to appear young and happy

77
00:03:57,400 --> 00:03:59,443
and vibrant all of the time.

78
00:03:59,443 --> 00:04:02,822
All that effort put towards pretending that you're not going through

79
00:04:02,822 --> 00:04:04,532
a natural process of aging

80
00:04:04,573 --> 00:04:06,659
is a complete waste of time.

81
00:04:07,159 --> 00:04:10,955
Meanwhile, a day in the life of a peri- or postmenopausal woman

82
00:04:10,997 --> 00:04:14,500
could be running to the bathroom to wait out a hot flush,

83
00:04:14,542 --> 00:04:17,753
looking for paper towels for perspiration that came out of nowhere

84
00:04:17,753 --> 00:04:19,672
when everyone else was freezing,

85
00:04:19,672 --> 00:04:22,049
skipping a meeting or sending someone else,

86
00:04:22,049 --> 00:04:25,386
because a wave of anxiety made you feel like you couldn't cope

87
00:04:25,386 --> 00:04:27,596
or skipping a business trip altogether,

88
00:04:27,638 --> 00:04:31,600
because heavy periods made leaving home virtually impossible.

89
00:04:32,143 --> 00:04:36,480
One way to raise awareness is to bring the discussion right into the workplace.

90
00:04:36,522 --> 00:04:39,066
Many companies already offer training programs

91
00:04:39,108 --> 00:04:41,444
on things like diversity and inclusion,

92
00:04:41,485 --> 00:04:42,737
anti-harassment,

93
00:04:42,737 --> 00:04:45,573
conversations on mental health or parental leave.

94
00:04:46,032 --> 00:04:48,659
Let's normalize conversation on menopause,

95
00:04:48,701 --> 00:04:51,245
inviting people of all genders and all ages

96
00:04:51,245 --> 00:04:55,249
to understand what's happening in this natural process of aging,

97
00:04:55,249 --> 00:04:57,585
so people can learn how to be supportive.

98
00:04:58,711 --> 00:05:03,424
Next is the physical setup and expectations around work culture.

99
00:05:03,466 --> 00:05:08,637
For example, open-plan offices are a disaster for so many reasons,

100
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,765
not being able to control the temperature,

101
00:05:10,806 --> 00:05:13,601
having no doors to close when you're having a hot flush

102
00:05:13,642 --> 00:05:15,853
or need a moment to regroup.

103
00:05:15,853 --> 00:05:19,065
And, of course, you can't change an entire floor plan overnight.

104
00:05:19,106 --> 00:05:21,567
But there probably are some things you can do,

105
00:05:21,567 --> 00:05:25,488
whether that's desk fans, availability of period products,

106
00:05:25,488 --> 00:05:29,200
letting someone move their desk from closest to the radiator

107
00:05:29,241 --> 00:05:32,495
or having a few rooms with a few doors that you can close

108
00:05:32,495 --> 00:05:34,205
if you need a moment to regroup

109
00:05:34,205 --> 00:05:36,123
are all good ideas.

110
00:05:36,123 --> 00:05:39,418
In situations where women wear uniforms at work,

111
00:05:39,418 --> 00:05:41,545
updating the cut to a thinner fabric,

112
00:05:41,545 --> 00:05:44,298
making it more breathable or even sweat-wicking

113
00:05:44,340 --> 00:05:46,842
can make a huge difference to a woman's comfort,

114
00:05:46,884 --> 00:05:48,677
as well as having a few extra around,

115
00:05:48,719 --> 00:05:50,888
if a quick change is required during the day.

116
00:05:51,555 --> 00:05:53,849
For employees who can work remotely,

117
00:05:53,891 --> 00:05:57,686
you can make it easier by giving examples of things to say to a manager

118
00:05:57,686 --> 00:05:59,271
when symptoms are out of control.

119
00:05:59,313 --> 00:06:02,900
For example, "I'm having a really tough day due to my symptoms,

120
00:06:02,900 --> 00:06:06,821
and it's making it difficult for me to perform at my best in the office."

121
00:06:06,862 --> 00:06:08,739
In cases where that's not possible,

122
00:06:08,781 --> 00:06:10,658
being able to say things like,

123
00:06:10,699 --> 00:06:15,329
"I need to take a few more frequent breaks today rather than one long break,"

124
00:06:15,329 --> 00:06:19,792
or "Hold on a second -- I'm having a hot flush."

125
00:06:19,834 --> 00:06:23,671
I truly believe that more openness about this topic,

126
00:06:23,671 --> 00:06:27,967
as well as leniency from an office around scheduling or more frequent breaks

127
00:06:28,008 --> 00:06:30,678
will make a huge difference to retaining women

128
00:06:30,678 --> 00:06:33,055
and getting them over that next promotion hurdle.

129
00:06:33,806 --> 00:06:35,224
And then there's health care,

130
00:06:35,224 --> 00:06:37,017
which is perhaps the most important.

131
00:06:37,059 --> 00:06:38,227
In many cases,

132
00:06:38,227 --> 00:06:41,689
a workplace will provide in-part or in-full health care to its employees.

133
00:06:41,730 --> 00:06:44,984
So there's an opportunity to include menopause directly.

134
00:06:44,984 --> 00:06:48,320
It can benefit a woman to have access to real information

135
00:06:48,362 --> 00:06:50,406
about what treatment options are available

136
00:06:50,448 --> 00:06:53,200
and what doctors or specialists she may want to see,

137
00:06:53,242 --> 00:06:55,953
whether that's gynecologists, endocrinologists,

138
00:06:55,995 --> 00:06:57,204
pelvic floor physios

139
00:06:57,204 --> 00:06:58,873
or talk therapists.

140
00:06:58,914 --> 00:07:03,461
Menopause is a highly complex and individualized situation,

141
00:07:03,461 --> 00:07:06,255
and having access to high-quality health care providers

142
00:07:06,297 --> 00:07:08,591
that can talk you through your treatment options

143
00:07:08,632 --> 00:07:10,926
and help you figure out what you want to do

144
00:07:10,926 --> 00:07:12,386
is critical.

145
00:07:12,386 --> 00:07:14,805
In many cases, insurance does cover this,

146
00:07:14,847 --> 00:07:17,850
or national health systems do have this available,

147
00:07:17,892 --> 00:07:19,477
but it can be hard to navigate,

148
00:07:19,518 --> 00:07:23,063
and all of the symptoms make finding the right doctor time-consuming

149
00:07:23,105 --> 00:07:24,857
and energy-draining.

150
00:07:24,899 --> 00:07:26,901
Just as we have health care resources

151
00:07:26,942 --> 00:07:30,613
for what to expect when you're having a child and how to be a great parent,

152
00:07:30,654 --> 00:07:34,492
we need better health care resources for what to expect in perimenopause

153
00:07:34,492 --> 00:07:36,702
and support through the entire transition.

154
00:07:37,244 --> 00:07:39,079
Every workplace is different,

155
00:07:39,121 --> 00:07:42,958
and not all companies will have the same approach to becoming menopause friendly.

156
00:07:43,334 --> 00:07:44,710
But at the very least,

157
00:07:44,752 --> 00:07:49,465
increasing awareness and demonstrating some real empathy can be low-cost.

158
00:07:49,882 --> 00:07:53,511
Just this year, we've heard several corporations make announcements

159
00:07:53,552 --> 00:07:56,847
about boosting female leadership at the top -- and that's great.

160
00:07:56,889 --> 00:07:59,683
And it will take a lot of coordinated efforts to get there.

161
00:07:59,725 --> 00:08:01,560
But what's the first step?

162
00:08:01,602 --> 00:08:03,771
Getting serious about menopause.

163
00:08:04,313 --> 00:08:05,564
Thank you.

Full English transcript to translate (translate ALL cues, do not stop until the last one):

1
00:00:01,873 --> 00:00:07,045
Of America's 500 largest companies, only 42 have female CEOs.

2
00:00:07,086 --> 00:00:08,796
And if you look at other countries,

3
00:00:08,838 --> 00:00:11,424
the data is similar, and in some cases, worse.

4
00:00:11,924 --> 00:00:14,052
And, of course, there are a number of factors

5
00:00:14,052 --> 00:00:16,929
contributing to global gender inequity in the workplace.

6
00:00:16,971 --> 00:00:20,683
But I believe that there is one factor often underecognized,

7
00:00:20,725 --> 00:00:22,268
and that's menopause.

8
00:00:22,894 --> 00:00:24,479
So what do I mean?

9
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:27,315
Many women will rise into senior positions of leadership

10
00:00:27,356 --> 00:00:29,192
in their 40s or their 50s.

11
00:00:29,192 --> 00:00:31,736
The average age of a CEO is mid-50s.

12
00:00:32,153 --> 00:00:35,364
Perimenopause, or the transition into menopause,

13
00:00:35,406 --> 00:00:39,535
generally occurs also between 45 and 55.

14
00:00:39,535 --> 00:00:41,621
And this is where the symptoms really start,

15
00:00:41,621 --> 00:00:43,498
and it can last up to 10 years.

16
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335
So just as a woman is stepping up, taking the reins,

17
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,170
earning at her highest potential,

18
00:00:49,212 --> 00:00:51,297
her body can feel like it's betraying her.

19
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:55,134
Now, for anyone who doesn't know, which turns out to be many of us --

20
00:00:55,176 --> 00:00:58,471
for me, I had my first conversation with my own mother about menopause

21
00:00:58,471 --> 00:01:00,056
in the last few years --

22
00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,724
it's a big deal.

23
00:01:01,724 --> 00:01:04,685
And it's not just a big deal for women over 45.

24
00:01:04,685 --> 00:01:07,605
Nonbinary, transmasculine and younger women

25
00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233
may all cope with menopausal symptoms.

26
00:01:10,775 --> 00:01:12,401
So what are these symptoms?

27
00:01:13,027 --> 00:01:14,237
They can be physical,

28
00:01:14,237 --> 00:01:20,201
so that's hot flushes, joint pain, urinary incontinence, heavy periods.

29
00:01:20,201 --> 00:01:23,746
They can also be mental: anxiety, depression,

30
00:01:23,788 --> 00:01:26,916
bouts of low confidence, difficulty sleeping.

31
00:01:26,958 --> 00:01:30,253
Imagine that -- 10 years of difficulty sleeping.

32
00:01:31,129 --> 00:01:34,257
The list is long and symptoms can change,

33
00:01:34,298 --> 00:01:36,592
so it means you never really know what to expect.

34
00:01:37,051 --> 00:01:39,554
But make no mistake: they are significant.

35
00:01:39,554 --> 00:01:44,058
So significant, that in a study by Vodafone across five countries,

36
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,062
it found that 60 percent of women dealing with menopausal symptoms

37
00:01:48,062 --> 00:01:50,314
said it impacted their work.

38
00:01:50,356 --> 00:01:52,608
In another study in the UK,

39
00:01:52,650 --> 00:01:57,238
30 percent of women said they missed work due to their symptoms.

40
00:01:57,280 --> 00:02:01,450
And at the extremes, women are making serious choices about their careers.

41
00:02:01,450 --> 00:02:04,871
As many as 11 percent forewent a promotion opportunity,

42
00:02:04,912 --> 00:02:08,207
and as many as eight percent resigned from their positions

43
00:02:08,249 --> 00:02:10,126
because of menopausal symptoms.

44
00:02:10,585 --> 00:02:14,380
My fascination with female leadership and the obvious gap

45
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:18,259
began as a strategy consultant working in boardrooms around the world.

46
00:02:18,301 --> 00:02:20,928
In over 10 years, I can count on one hand

47
00:02:20,970 --> 00:02:25,975
the number of senior women over 50 in executive positions that I saw,

48
00:02:26,017 --> 00:02:29,770
whether it was Seattle or Dubai, Lagos or upstate New York,

49
00:02:29,812 --> 00:02:31,397
women were scarce at the top.

50
00:02:32,565 --> 00:02:35,860
I started to look around and question the system I was in,

51
00:02:35,902 --> 00:02:38,529
wonder what opportunities really existed.

52
00:02:38,571 --> 00:02:41,199
The women at the top were exceptional.

53
00:02:41,199 --> 00:02:43,492
The bar seemed almost too high to reach.

54
00:02:43,993 --> 00:02:48,456
And so I started speaking to my friends and colleagues in their 40s and 50s.

55
00:02:48,456 --> 00:02:52,793
They talked about things I knew: balancing a serious job, teenagers

56
00:02:52,835 --> 00:02:55,004
and caring for elderly parents.

57
00:02:55,004 --> 00:02:58,382
But in a few instances, close friends opened up.

58
00:02:58,424 --> 00:03:01,552
They talked about how their bodies and their minds were changing

59
00:03:01,552 --> 00:03:05,097
and the impact of menopause on many aspects of their lives,

60
00:03:05,139 --> 00:03:06,349
including work.

61
00:03:07,391 --> 00:03:09,435
It was at that point that I realized

62
00:03:09,477 --> 00:03:12,188
if I wanted to make a difference to women in the workplace

63
00:03:12,230 --> 00:03:15,274
so we can all work as long as we want to,

64
00:03:15,274 --> 00:03:17,151
it was to improve menopause care.

65
00:03:18,110 --> 00:03:20,613
I believe the workplace can play a major role

66
00:03:20,655 --> 00:03:24,533
in improving the experience for women coping with menopausal symptoms.

67
00:03:24,533 --> 00:03:28,120
And it might sound cliché, but it does start with awareness.

68
00:03:28,162 --> 00:03:31,791
And then it means changing the physical setup of our offices,

69
00:03:31,832 --> 00:03:34,293
resetting expectations around work culture

70
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,254
and updating health care policies.

71
00:03:36,671 --> 00:03:38,756
So, first: awareness.

72
00:03:38,798 --> 00:03:42,593
Since we learn virtually nothing about menopause in school,

73
00:03:42,593 --> 00:03:44,762
in the news, in pop culture,

74
00:03:44,804 --> 00:03:48,849
it's impossible to expect that even the most well-intentioned manager

75
00:03:48,891 --> 00:03:51,143
would have a clue how to be supportive.

76
00:03:51,644 --> 00:03:57,358
So many societies place so much pressure on women to appear young and happy

77
00:03:57,400 --> 00:03:59,443
and vibrant all of the time.

78
00:03:59,443 --> 00:04:02,822
All that effort put towards pretending that you're not going through

79
00:04:02,822 --> 00:04:04,532
a natural process of aging

80
00:04:04,573 --> 00:04:06,659
is a complete waste of time.

81
00:04:07,159 --> 00:04:10,955
Meanwhile, a day in the life of a peri- or postmenopausal woman

82
00:04:10,997 --> 00:04:14,500
could be running to the bathroom to wait out a hot flush,

83
00:04:14,542 --> 00:04:17,753
looking for paper towels for perspiration that came out of nowhere

84
00:04:17,753 --> 00:04:19,672
when everyone else was freezing,

85
00:04:19,672 --> 00:04:22,049
skipping a meeting or sending someone else,

86
00:04:22,049 --> 00:04:25,386
because a wave of anxiety made you feel like you couldn't cope

87
00:04:25,386 --> 00:04:27,596
or skipping a business trip altogether,

88
00:04:27,638 --> 00:04:31,600
because heavy periods made leaving home virtually impossible.

89
00:04:32,143 --> 00:04:36,480
One way to raise awareness is to bring the discussion right into the workplace.

90
00:04:36,522 --> 00:04:39,066
Many companies already offer training programs

91
00:04:39,108 --> 00:04:41,444
on things like diversity and inclusion,

92
00:04:41,485 --> 00:04:42,737
anti-harassment,

93
00:04:42,737 --> 00:04:45,573
conversations on mental health or parental leave.

94
00:04:46,032 --> 00:04:48,659
Let's normalize conversation on menopause,

95
00:04:48,701 --> 00:04:51,245
inviting people of all genders and all ages

96
00:04:51,245 --> 00:04:55,249
to understand what's happening in this natural process of aging,

97
00:04:55,249 --> 00:04:57,585
so people can learn how to be supportive.

98
00:04:58,711 --> 00:05:03,424
Next is the physical setup and expectations around work culture.

99
00:05:03,466 --> 00:05:08,637
For example, open-plan offices are a disaster for so many reasons,

100
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,765
not being able to control the temperature,

1  
00:00:01,873 --> 00:00:07,045  
在美国500强企业中，仅有42家由女性担任CEO。  

2  
00:00:07,086 --> 00:00:08,796  
如果看看其他国家的数据，  

3  
00:00:08,838 --> 00:00:11,424  
情况相似，有些地方甚至更糟。  

4  
00:00:11,924 --> 00:00:14,052  
导致职场性别的不平等，  

5  
00:00:14,052 --> 00:00:16,929  
背后显然有众多因素。  

6  
00:00:16,971 --> 00:00:20,683  
但有件事我总觉得被严重忽视——  

7  
00:00:20,725 --> 00:00:22,268  
那就是更年期。  

8  
00:00:22,894 --> 00:00:24,479  
为什么这么说呢？  

9  
00:00:24,520 --> 00:00:27,315  
很多女性往往在40到50多岁  

10  
00:00:27,356 --> 00:00:29,192  
登上领导岗位。  

11  
00:00:29,192 --> 00:00:31,736  
CEO的平均年龄是50出头。  

12  
00:00:32,153 --> 00:00:35,364  
而围绝经期（更年期过渡期）  

13  
00:00:35,406 --> 00:00:39,535  
正巧也出现在45到55岁之间。  

14  
00:00:39,535 --> 00:00:41,621  
而症状往往从这里开始爆发，  

15  
00:00:41,621 --> 00:00:43,498  
持续可达10年。  

16  
00:00:43,498 --> 00:00:47,335  
当你正抓住机会晋升，收入攀上巅峰时，  

17  
00:00:47,376 --> 00:00:49,170  
身体却可能开始背叛你。  

18  
00:00:49,212 --> 00:00:51,297  

19  
00:00:51,839 --> 00:00:55,134  
对绝大多数人来说——包括我自己，  

20  
00:00:55,176 --> 00:01:00,056  
直到几年前提及与母亲谈论更年期这件事——  

21  
00:01:00,056 --> 00:01:01,724  
这其实是个大问题。  

22  
00:01:01,724 --> 00:01:04,685  
而且不只是45岁以上的女性要面对。  

23  
00:01:04,685 --> 00:01:07,605  
非二元性别者、跨男群体，甚至更年轻的女性  

24  
00:01:07,647 --> 00:01:10,233  
都可能受更年期症状困扰。  

25  
00:01:10,775 --> 00:01:12,401  
具体有哪些症状？  

26  
00:01:13,027 --> 00:01:14,237  有些是身体上的——  

27  
00:01:14,237 --> 00:01:20,201  如潮热、关节疼痛、尿失禁、月经过多。  

28  
00:01:20,201 --> 00:01:23,746  也可能是心理层面——  

29  
00:01:23,788 --> 00:01:26,916  焦虑、抑郁、自信下滑、失眠困扰。  

30  
00:01:26,958 --> 00:01:30,253  想象一下——十年难以入眠是什么体验？  

31  
00:01:31,129 --> 00:01:34,257  症状清单很长且变化多端，  

32  
00:01:34,298 --> 00:01:36,592  让人永远捉摸不透。  

33  
00:01:37,051 --> 00:01:39,554  但这些影响绝非微不足道，  

34  
00:01:39,554 --> 00:01:44,058  比如沃达丰在五国的研究表明，  

35  
00:01:44,100 --> 00:01:48,062  60%经历更年期症状的女性表示工作受影响。  

36  
00:01:48,062 --> 00:01:50,314  另一项英国研究显示，  

37  
00:01:50,356 --> 00:01:52,608  30%女性因症状请过假。  

38  
00:01:52,650 --> 00:01:57,238  极端案例中，女性甚至做出职业选择——  

39  
00:01:57,280 --> 00:02:01,450  如有11%放弃升职机会，8%选择离职。  

40  
00:02:01,450 --> 00:02:04,871  

41  
00:02:04,912 --> 00:02:08,207  

42  
00:02:08,249 --> 00:02:10,126  

43  
00:02:10,585 --> 00:02:14,380  我对女性领导层的研究始于担任战略顾问时，  

44  
00:02:14,380 --> 00:02:18,259  在全球众多董事会见证了一个普遍现象：  

45  
00:02:18,301 --> 00:02:25,975  能数清50岁以上女性高管的数量，不论是在西雅图、迪拜、拉各斯还是纽约州北部。  

46  
00:02:26,017 --> 00:02:29,770  

47  
00:02:29,812 --> 00:02:31,397  

48  
00:02:32,565 --> 00:02:35,860  我开始质疑所处的系统与机遇的真实性。  

49  
00:02:35,902 --> 00:02:38,529  最顶端的女性堪称精英，门槛几乎高不可攀。  

50  
00:02:38,571 --> 00:02:41,199  我开始访谈身边40到50岁的朋友和同事。  

51  
00:02:41,199 --> 00:02:43,492  她们谈论着熟悉的困境：兼顾工作、青春期孩子与照顾老人。  

52  
00:02:43,993 --> 00:02:48,456  但少数密友打开心扉，  

53  
00:02:48,456 --> 00:02:55,004  提及身体与心理的变化，以及更年期对生活的全方位冲击——  

54  
00:02:55,004 --> 00:02:58,382  

55  
00:02:58,424 --> 00:03:01,552  尤其是在工作层面。  

56  
00:03:01,552 --> 00:03:05,097  

57  
00:03:05,139 --> 00:03:06,349  

58  
00:03:07,391 --> 00:03:09,435  我突然意识到，  

59  
00:03:09,477 --> 00:03:12,188  若想让女性在职场获得持续发展，  

60  
00:03:12,230 --> 00:03:15,274  关键可能在于改善更年期支持。  

61  
00:03:15,274 --> 00:03:17,151  

62  
00:03:18,110 --> 00:03:20,613  我相信职场能发挥重大作用，  

63  
00:03:20,655 --> 00:03:24,533  提升更年期女性工作的体验感。  

64  
00:03:24,533 --> 00:03:28,120  这听起来可能老生常谈，但首先需提升认知。  

65  
00:03:28,162 --> 00:03:31,791  其次要改造办公环境，  

66  
00:03:31,832 --> 00:03:34,293  调整职场文化期待，  

67  
00:03:34,293 --> 00:03:36,254  并更新健康政策。  

68  
00:03:36,671 --> 00:03:38,756  首先是认知提升。  

69  
00:03:38,798 --> 00:03:42,593  因为学校、新闻、流行文化几乎不谈更年期，  

70  
000:03:42,593 --> 00:03:44,762  

71  
00:03:44,804 --> 00:03:48,849  即使善意管理者也可能不知如何支持。  

72  
00:03:48,891 --> 00:03:51,143  

73  
00:03:51,644 --> 00:03:57,358  许多社会要求女性永远保持年轻快乐、活力四射，  

74  
00:03:57,400 --> 00:03:59,443  而这种伪装自然衰老过程的挣扎  

75  
00:03:59,443 --> 00:04:02,822  

76  
00:04:02,822 --> 00:04:04,532  完全徒劳无益。  

77  
00:04:04,573 --> 00:04:06,659  

78  
00:04:07,159 --> 00:04:10,955  真实生活可能是这样的——  

79  
00:04:10,997 --> 00:04:14,500  冲向洗手间忍受潮热，  

80  
00:04:14,542 --> 00:04:17,753  挖找纸巾擦突然冒出的汗，  

81  
00:04:17,753 --> 00:04:19,672  抽冷子众人冻得发抖时自己却像被烤着，  

82  
00:04:19,672 --> 00:04:22,049  临时放弃会议或派他人代替，  

83  
00:04:22,049 --> 00:04:25,386  因突如其来的焦虑无法应对，  

84  
00:04:25,386 --> 00:04:27,596  或者直接请假不参加商务出差，  

85  
00:04:27,638 --> 00:04:31,600  因为月经过多让出门变得不可能。  

86  
00:04:32,143 --> 00:04:36,480  要提升认知，必须让职场主动讨论这个议题。  

87  
00:04:36,522 --> 00:04:39,066  许多企业已有培训项目，  

88  
00:04:39,108 --> 00:04:41,444  如多元文化、反骚扰、  

89  
00:04:41,485 --> 00:04:42,737  心理健康、育儿假等话题。  

90  
00:04:42,737 --> 00:04:45,573  

91  
00:04:46,032 --> 00:04:48,659  我们要正常化更年期对话，  

92  
00:04:48,701 --> 00:04:51,245  邀请各性别、各年龄员工了解这个自然过程，  

93  
00:04:51,245 --> 00:04:55,249  从而学会如何支持他人。  

94  
00:04:55,249 --> 00:04:57,585  

95  
00:04:58,711 --> 00:05:03,424  其次是改造办公环境与文化预期。  

96  
00:05:03,466 --> 00:05:08,637  比如开放办公空间简直是灾难——  

97  
00:05:08,679 --> 00:05:10,765  温度无法调控，

title: If trees could speak
en_source:
1
00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:03,216
Humans do not see trees.

2
00:00:03,240 --> 00:00:05,476
They walk by us every day.

3
00:00:05,500 --> 00:00:09,507
They sit and sleep, smoke and picnic

4
00:00:09,531 --> 00:00:11,910
and secretly kiss in our shade.

5
00:00:11,934 --> 00:00:15,436
They pluck our leaves and gorge on our fruits.

6
00:00:15,460 --> 00:00:17,166
They break our branches

7
00:00:17,190 --> 00:00:21,206
or carve their lover's name on our trunks with their blades

8
00:00:21,230 --> 00:00:23,493
and vow eternal love.

9
00:00:24,130 --> 00:00:26,616
They weave necklaces out of our needles

10
00:00:26,640 --> 00:00:29,386
and paint our flowers into art.

11
00:00:29,410 --> 00:00:32,726
They split us into logs to heat their homes,

12
00:00:32,750 --> 00:00:35,076
and sometimes they chop us down

13
00:00:35,100 --> 00:00:38,266
just because they think we obstruct their view.

14
00:00:38,290 --> 00:00:43,452
They make cradles, wine corks, chewing gum, rustic furniture

15
00:00:43,476 --> 00:00:46,970
and produce the most beautiful music out of us.

16
00:00:47,360 --> 00:00:49,226
And they turn us into books

17
00:00:49,250 --> 00:00:53,246
in which they bury themselves on cold winter nights.

18
00:00:53,270 --> 00:00:58,606
They use our wood to manufacture coffins in which they end their lives.

19
00:00:58,630 --> 00:01:02,836
And they even compose the most romantic poems for us,

20
00:01:02,860 --> 00:01:07,356
claiming we're the link between earth and sky.

21
00:01:07,380 --> 00:01:09,923
And yet, they do not see us.

22
00:01:12,790 --> 00:01:16,516
So one of the many beauties of the art of storytelling

23
00:01:16,540 --> 00:01:20,566
is to imagine yourself inside someone else's voice.

24
00:01:20,590 --> 00:01:25,306
But as writers, as much as we love stories and words,

25
00:01:25,330 --> 00:01:29,606
I believe we must also be interested in silences:

26
00:01:29,630 --> 00:01:33,836
the things we cannot talk about easily in our societies,

27
00:01:33,860 --> 00:01:36,946
the marginalized, the disempowered.

28
00:01:36,970 --> 00:01:41,026
In that sense, literature can, and hopefully does,

29
00:01:41,050 --> 00:01:43,816
bring the periphery to the center,

30
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:46,636
make the invisible a bit more visible,

31
00:01:46,660 --> 00:01:49,416
make the unheard a bit more heard,

32
00:01:49,440 --> 00:01:55,275
and empathy and understanding speak louder than demagoguery and apathy.

33
00:01:55,974 --> 00:01:58,565
Stories bring us together.

34
00:01:58,589 --> 00:02:03,560
Untold stories and entrenched silences keep us apart.

35
00:02:04,130 --> 00:02:08,606
But how to tell the stories of humanity and nature

36
00:02:08,630 --> 00:02:11,666
at a time when our planet is burning

37
00:02:11,690 --> 00:02:13,456
and there is no precedent

38
00:02:13,480 --> 00:02:16,556
for what we're about to experience collectively

39
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:20,863
whether it's political, social or ecological?

40
00:02:21,500 --> 00:02:23,006
But tell we must

41
00:02:23,030 --> 00:02:24,496
because if there's one thing

42
00:02:24,520 --> 00:02:27,646
that is destroying our world more than anything,

43
00:02:27,670 --> 00:02:29,276
it is numbness.

44
00:02:29,300 --> 00:02:35,056
When people become disconnected, desensitized, indifferent,

45
00:02:35,080 --> 00:02:38,539
when they stop listening, when they stop learning

46
00:02:38,563 --> 00:02:40,296
and when they stop caring

47
00:02:40,320 --> 00:02:44,653
about what's happening here, there and everywhere.

48
00:02:48,430 --> 00:02:52,196
We measure time differently, trees and humans.

49
00:02:52,220 --> 00:02:54,066
Human time is linear --

50
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:55,463
a neat continuum

51
00:02:55,487 --> 00:03:00,271
stretching from a past that is deemed to be over and done with

52
00:03:00,295 --> 00:03:04,860
towards the future that is supposed to be pristine, untouched.

53
00:03:05,240 --> 00:03:07,269
Tree time is circular.

54
00:03:07,293 --> 00:03:12,066
Both the past and the future breathe within the present moment.

55
00:03:12,090 --> 00:03:15,536
And the present does not move in one direction.

56
00:03:15,560 --> 00:03:19,146
Instead it draws circles within circles,

57
00:03:19,170 --> 00:03:23,063
like the rings you would find when you cut us down.

58
00:03:23,790 --> 00:03:28,526
Next time you walk by a tree, try to slow down and listen

59
00:03:28,550 --> 00:03:31,546
because each of us whispers in the wind.

60
00:03:31,570 --> 00:03:32,744
Look at us.

61
00:03:32,768 --> 00:03:35,396
We're older than you and your kind.

62
00:03:35,420 --> 00:03:37,769
Listen to what we have to tell,

63
00:03:37,793 --> 00:03:43,700
because hidden inside our story is the past and the future of humanity.
prompt:
You are a professional translator. Translate the following English TED Talk
transcript into Chinese.

The transcript is in SRT subtitle format. Each cue block consists of:
  1. a cue number
  2. a timestamp line (HH:MM:SS,mmm --> HH:MM:SS,mmm)
  3. one or more lines of text

CRITICAL - completeness (read first):
- You MUST translate EVERY SINGLE cue, from the first to the very last one in the input.
- Your output MUST contain exactly as many cue blocks as the input. Do not stop early.
- NEVER skip, summarize, abbreviate, or collapse cues. Translate each one in full.
- NEVER write meta-comments such as "(the rest is kept as in the original)",
  "(remaining cues continue in the same manner)", "..." or any placeholder in place
  of a real translation. Every cue must contain the actual translated text.
- If the transcript is long, keep going regardless — finish all cues.

Rules:
- Preserve every cue number exactly as it appears
- Preserve every timestamp line exactly as it appears
- Translate only the text lines of each cue
- Keep the same SRT structure (cue number, timestamp line, text) for every cue
- Where appropriate, choose informal, colloquial terms over formal or academic ones.
- Choose modern terms and phrases over traditional ones. Translators should be well-versed in the topics covered.
- Strive to match the tone and flow of the speaker's original talk. Rather than produce a word-for-word translation, aim to find the color, energy and "poetry" in the speaker's organic style and try to emulate it.
- Choose words and phrases that are most universally understood among all dialects.
- Instead of a word-for-word translation, try finding a similar expression in the target language. If no equivalent exists, opt for the translation that readers will find least confusing, even if it is less colorful than the original.
- TED is always written as "TED" and should not be translated.
- For books, movies, magazines and poems, check if the work has an official translation in your language; if not, don't translate the title.
- If the target language uses a non-Latin alphabet, transliterate people's names. For places, use the name that is most common in your language. Otherwise, transliterate.
- Use the target language's native punctuation.
- Use standard unicode characters and avoid those that are platform-specific.
- You may convert units of measurement to make them more understandable to viewers in your language.
- Preserve all stage directions in parentheses, e.g. (Laughter), (Applause)
  — translate them into Chinese as well
- Do not translate proper names (people, places, organizations)
- Your final answer must be the translated SRT text ONLY — no introduction, no
  commentary, no explanation before or after it (any reasoning stays out of the answer)

Reference — the first cues of the official TED Chinese translation of this talk are shown below. Use them as a style guide for tone, vocabulary, and translation choices, but do NOT copy them verbatim into your output unless they correspond to the same cue numbers:

人類通常當樹係空氣，

2

每日佢地都會行過我哋身邊。

3
00:00:05,516 --> 00:00:09,516
佢地係我哋嘅身影下，

4
00:00:09,516 --> 00:00:11,916
坐低休息、訓教、 野餐、偷偷錫嘴。

5
00:00:11,916 --> 00:00:15,436
佢哋掹我哋啲葉， 貪圖我哋結嘅果實。
