qwq:
好，我现在需要将用户提供的英文TED演讲字幕翻译成中文，并且严格按照他们的指令来执行。首先，我得仔细阅读用户的所有指示，确保完全理解每一个要求。用户特别强调必须翻译每一个字幕块，不能遗漏或缩短。这一点非常关键，因为如果错过了任何一个cue，都会导致整个翻译不完整。

首先，我会先看用户提供的参考翻译，即前五个cue的例子，以了解他们期望的风格和用词。例如，第一个cue翻译成“人類通常當樹係空氣”，这里用了比较口语化的表达，比如“係”代替“是”，“空氣”作为“see”的翻译，可能是指人类对树木视而不见，像空气一样忽略。这给了我很强的提示，要使用较为口语化且自然的中文表达，而不是字对字的直译。

接下来，我需要处理整个字幕列表，每个cue都要逐个处理。比如第一个cue：
1
00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:03,216
Humans do not see trees.

根据参考翻译，这里的“see”可能不是字面的“看到”，而是“意识到”或“注意到”，所以翻译成“人類通常當樹係空氣”中的“當樹係空氣”是比喻，表示视而不见。需要保持这种比喻风格。

接下来是第二个cue，要确保时码不变，只翻译文本部分。例如：
2
00:00:03,240 --> 00:00:05,476
They walk by us every day.

参考翻译是“每日佢地都會行過我哋身邊。”这里使用了粤语的“佢地”和“行過”，可能用户希望使用某种方言？不过用户要求的是普遍易懂的词汇，所以可能需要再检查是否应该用普通话还是方言。不过参考例子用的看起来像粤语，但用户提到要现代用语，可能需要确认。这里可能用户提供的参考是粤语，但用户指令中的输出可能需要普通话？这点要注意。不过根据问题描述，用户提供的参考前几个cue的中文是粤语，但用户的任务中提到“用目标语言的标点符号”，可能目标语言是中文普通话，所以可能需要再斟酌。

不过仔细看用户的问题，指出“CRITICAL”部分需要每个cue都翻译，且参考例子可能只是样式，所以可能需要按照普通话进行翻译，而用户给出的参考例子可能是一个例子，但用户可能希望使用普通话，或者根据实际情况看用户提供的例子可能为粤语。但题目里说“SRT subtitles”，而用户给的参考翻译是粤语，比如“人類通常當樹係空氣”，所以可能该TED演讲的中文翻译是粤语？这可能需要按照用户提供的参考例子来，即使用粤语。但需要再次确认用户的要求。

但用户的问题里提到“翻译成Chinese”，而参考例子是粤语，所以可能存在用户需要的是粤语翻译。但用户可能属于香港地区的需求？或者需要进一步确认。但根据问题中的指示，用户可能希望遵循给定参考翻译的风格，即可能使用粤语。例如参考中的“係”和“都會”是粤语用法，比如“每日佢地都會行過我哋身邊。”，其中“都會”在普通话是“都会”，但粤语也用这个词。所以可能用户希望使用粤语。

因此，我需要根据用户给出的参考翻译作为样式，采用类似粤语的表达方式，但要注意用词的选择和口语化。

接下来，逐个cue翻译：

比如cue6：
6
00:00:15,460 --> 00:00:17,166
They break our branches

需要翻译成“佢哋折斷我哋嘅枝條。” 或者更自然的粤语表达。参考用户之前的风格，例如第5cue的翻译用了“掟我哋啲葉”，所以可能用“折”的动词，保持动作描述。

而cue7：
7
00:00:17,190 --> 00:00:21,206
or carve their lover's name on our trunks with their blades
and vow eternal love.

这句较长，需要分成两段，注意粤语的连贯性。翻译可能是“或者用刀在我哋嘅樹幹刻下情人嘅名，再發誓至死不渝。” 或者更准确的结构，但要确保符合粤语的表达习惯。

接下来要确保每个cue的cue号和时间戳完全一致，只翻译文本部分。

处理到后面的时间点，比如cue8：
8
00:00:21,230 --> 00:00:23,493
and vow eternal love.

需要翻译成“再發誓至死不渝。” 这里“vow eternal love”是“誓言永恒的爱”，粤语中可能会用“至死不渝”来表达。

继续处理后面的cue，比如cue11：
11
00:00:29,410 --> 00:00:32,726
They split us into logs to heat their homes,

这里用“split into logs”可以翻译为“將我哋劈成原木，用嚟為佢哋嘅屋企取暖”。“split”是劈开，logs是原木，home是屋企。

cue12：
12
00:00:32,750 --> 00:00:35,076
and sometimes they chop us down
just because they think we obstruct their view.

需要翻译成“或者只因覺得我哋遮住佢哋嘅视线，直接將我哋伐低。” 这里“chop us down”是“伐低”或“砍低”，“obstruct their view”是遮挡视线。

处理到后面的cue，如cue14：
14
00:00:38,290 --> 00:00:43,452
They make cradles, wine corks, chewing gum, rustic furniture

翻译成“佢哋用我哋製造搖籃、酒塞、嚼 gum 、老式傢俬”，但chewing gum在粤语可能用“香口膠”会更合适，但用户可能希望保留原词“chewing gum”，或者按官方翻译？

但根据用户指示，对专有名词的翻译如书籍、电影等如果没有官方翻译则保留原文，但“chewing gum”属于日常物品，可能需要翻译为“香口膠”。

然后检查时间戳是否完全保留，确保每个cue的编号、时间和文本正确无误。例如cue48：
48
00:02:48,430 --> 00:02:52,196
We measure time differently, trees and humans.

翻译成“树木同人類嘅時間概念唔同。” 这里“differently”翻译为“唔同”，结构调整为粤语表达。

在翻译过程中，需要注意保持每个cue的连贯性，并且按照用户参考示例的风格，采用口语化和现代用语。例如，cue58：
58
00:03:23,790 --> 00:03:28,526
Next time you walk by a tree, try to slow down and listen

可能需要翻译为“下次你途經一棵樹嘅時候，試吓放慢腳步傾聽”或者更自然的表达。

另外，用户指出“stage directions in parentheses, e.g. (Laughter), (Applause)”需要翻译，并保留括号。例如，如果出现括号内容如（笑声），则翻译成（笑声）或（鼓掌），但用户例子中的参考没有这种情况，所以可能该片段中没有，但需要在翻译时注意。

最后，确保所有cue都被翻译，不遗漏，并且遵循严格的格式要求，没有多余解释或注释。这需要逐一核对每个cue，确保翻译正确，同时保持结构无误。

遇到不确定的用词时，比如cue17：“they bury themselves on cold winter nights.”，可能翻译为“在寒冷嘅冬天夜晚自埋進書本裡。” 这里“bury themselves”是“沉溺于”或“沉浸在”。

还要注意单位转换，但这里没有明显的单位问题。专有名词如TED不需要翻译，保留。

完成所有cue的翻译后，再次通读检查，确保没有遗漏，每个cue的编号和时间戳都正确保留，文本部分准确传达原意，同时符合粤语口语的流畅表达。
</think>

1
00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:03,216
人類通常當樹係空氣，

2
00:00:03,240 --> 00:00:05,476
每日佢地都會行過我哋身邊。

3
00:00:05,500 --> 00:00:09,507
佢哋坐低、成眠、吸煙、搞野餐，

4
00:00:09,531 --> 00:00:11,910
成日偷啲親嘴都係係我哋陰涼下做。

5
00:00:11,934 --> 00:00:15,436
佢哋掟走我哋嘅葉，掟佢埋我哋嘅果兒。

6
00:00:15,460 --> 00:00:17,166
佢哋折斷我哋嘅枝條，

7
00:00:17,190 --> 00:00:21,206
用刀片喺我哋嘅樹乾刻滿戀人姓名

8
00:00:21,230 --> 00:00:23,493
再發誓要永遠愛護。

9
00:00:24,130 --> 00:00:26,616
佢哋用我哋嘅松針編成項鍊

10
00:00:26,640 --> 00:00:29,386
又把我哋嘅花畫成藝術品。

11
00:00:29,410 --> 00:00:32,726
將我哋劈成原木用嚟為佢哋嘅屋企取暖，

12
00:00:32,750 --> 00:00:35,076
或者只因覺得我哋遮住佢哋嘅视线，直接將我哋伐低。

13
00:00:35,100 --> 00:00:38,266
將我哋製成嬰兒床、酒塞、香口膠、民俗傢俬

14
00:00:38,290 --> 00:00:43,452
甚至造出我哋人生最優美嘅音樂。

15
00:00:43,476 --> 00:00:46,970
佢哋亦將我哋變成書本

16
00:00:47,360 --> 00:00:49,226
於寒夜埋身其中閲讀。

17
00:00:49,250 --> 00:00:53,246
用我哋嘅木材打造棺材結束生命

18
00:00:53,270 --> 00:00:58,606
（更寫下浪漫情詩歌頌我哋）

19
00:00:58,630 --> 00:01:02,836
堅稱我哋係連繫天地嘅橋樑。

20
00:01:02,860 --> 00:01:07,356
但人類仍然唔睇到我哋存在。

21
00:01:07,380 --> 00:01:09,923
即使经历咗咁多嘢，仍然睇唔到我哋。

22
00:01:12,790 --> 00:01:16,516
故事創作嘅美妙之處

23
00:01:16,540 --> 00:01:20,566
係可以代入到他人嘅身份思考。

24
00:01:20,590 --> 00:01:25,306
但做作家嘅同時，無論多著迷於文字與故事

25
00:01:25,330 --> 00:01:29,606
我哋都要多留意沉默嘅聲音：

26
00:01:29,630 --> 00:01:33,836
社會唔敢談論嘅敏感議題，

27
00:01:33,860 --> 00:01:36,946
邊個邊被邊個邊壓搾。

28
00:01:36,970 --> 00:01:41,026
文學嘅力量

29
00:01:41,050 --> 00:01:43,816
就係將邊界化作核心，

30
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:46,636
令不可見嘅嘢被察覺，

31
00:01:46,660 --> 00:01:49,416
讓無人傾聽嘅嘢被傾聽，

32
00:01:49,440 --> 00:01:55,275
同埋用同理心同理解力，戰勝冷漠同叫囂。

33
00:01:55,974 --> 00:01:58,565
故事令人類連繫。

34
00:01:58,589 --> 00:02:03,560
而沉默同未說出嘅故事，卻阻隔咗人哋。

35
00:02:04,130 --> 00:02:08,606
但點樣在地球面對極端環境、政治社會劇變時

36
00:02:08,630 --> 00:02:11,666
仍要講出人類同自然界嘅故事？

37
00:02:11,690 --> 00:02:13,456
尤其當今人類社會未有過嘅危機，

38
00:02:13,480 --> 00:02:16,556
無論係生態、政治定社會層面。

39
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:20,863
但我們必須嘗試講述

40
00:02:21,500 --> 00:02:23,006
係因呢個世界最嚴重嘅問題，

41
00:02:23,030 --> 00:02:24,496
正正係「麻木冷感」。

42
00:02:24,520 --> 00:02:27,646
當人類變得無視、漠不關心，

43
00:02:27,670 --> 00:02:29,276
當人人停止傾聽、放棄學習，

44
00:02:29,300 --> 00:02:35,056
亦不再關心世界呢度、埋面嘅悲劇，

45
00:02:35,080 --> 00:02:38,539
呢個社會就陷入最大嘅危機。

46
00:02:38,563 --> 00:02:40,296
（埋面都唔理）

47
00:00:40,320 --> 00:02:44,653
世界無論邊個角落發生嘅事。

48
00:02:48,430 --> 00:02:52,196
樹同人類嘅時間觀極之不同。

49
00:02:52,220 --> 00:02:54,066
人類視時間為線性---

50
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:55,463
由過去延伸向未來嘅單行線。

51
00:02:55,487 --> 00:03:00,271
認為過去已逝，未來純潔未受玷污，

52
00:03:00,295 --> 00:03:04,860
但棵樹同埋我哋嘅時間係循環嘅。

53
00:03:05,240 --> 00:03:07,269
過去同未來係共存我哋嘅現在。

54
00:03:07,293 --> 00:03:12,066
而現時並非單向前進，而係渦流漩轉---

55
00:03:12,090 --> 00:03:15,536
畫著一圈又一圈嘅圓

56
00:03:15,560 --> 00:03:19,146
就像切開棵樹見到嘅年輪咁。

57
00:03:19,170 --> 00:03:23,063
（嘥咗喺度見到嘅年輪）

58
00:03:23,790 --> 00:03:28,526
下回路經棵樹嘅時候，試下放慢腳步傾聽，

59
00:03:28,550 --> 00:03:31,546
因爲每棵樹風中喃喃自語，

60
00:03:31,570 --> 00:03:32,744
望住人哋睇下我哋---

61
00:03:32,768 --> 00:03:35,396
我哋比你同我哋人類更古老，

62
00:03:35,420 --> 00:03:37,769
真係傾聽我哋嘅故事，

63
00:03:37,793 --> 00:03:43,700
因爲埋住我哋嘅DNA，係人類嘅未來同過去。

title: How every child can thrive by five
en_source:
1
00:00:02,796 --> 00:00:04,556
[Baby cooing]

2
00:00:20,076 --> 00:00:22,796
What if I was to tell you

3
00:00:22,836 --> 00:00:27,756
that a game of peekaboo could change the world?

4
00:00:29,076 --> 00:00:31,356
Sounds impossible, right?

5
00:00:31,876 --> 00:00:35,756
Well, I’m here today to prove it’s not.

6
00:00:36,436 --> 00:00:38,116
Hi, I’m Molly and I’m seven.

7
00:00:38,156 --> 00:00:40,436
And this is my little friend, Ari.

8
00:00:40,636 --> 00:00:42,316
Say “Hi,” Ari.

9
00:00:43,276 --> 00:00:44,596
Hi.

10
00:00:45,116 --> 00:00:48,156
Oh, and this is my neighbor, Amarjot.

11
00:00:48,756 --> 00:00:53,036
He has to take Ari away now to get ready for our experiment.

12
00:00:53,156 --> 00:00:55,516
But don't worry, they'll be back.

13
00:00:56,916 --> 00:01:00,516
My talk today is about some powerful things you grownups can do.

14
00:01:00,556 --> 00:01:03,756
that shape us as children and the adults we become.

15
00:01:04,556 --> 00:01:06,116
How do I know?

16
00:01:06,156 --> 00:01:10,316
Because my parents and people around me did them early and often.

17
00:01:11,156 --> 00:01:13,396
I know not all kids are as lucky.

18
00:01:13,436 --> 00:01:17,716
Some of my friends, some kids at my school and many around the world.

19
00:01:18,196 --> 00:01:20,916
And I would really like to help change that.

20
00:01:22,116 --> 00:01:23,876
Thanks to scientists,

21
00:01:23,876 --> 00:01:27,236
we now know just how important the first five years are

22
00:01:27,276 --> 00:01:29,356
for our health and development,

23
00:01:29,396 --> 00:01:31,236
especially our brains.

24
00:01:31,276 --> 00:01:34,396
Ari started learning long before he was even born,

25
00:01:34,436 --> 00:01:36,316
from inside his mommy’s tummy.

26
00:01:36,716 --> 00:01:38,436
When Ari was born,

27
00:01:38,476 --> 00:01:43,236
he was tiny and he cried all the time.

28
00:01:43,356 --> 00:01:47,596
He was always hungry and he pooped a lot.

29
00:01:47,636 --> 00:01:49,436
[Laughter]

30
00:01:49,476 --> 00:01:53,236
Now he laughs and giggles and makes funny noises.

31
00:01:53,276 --> 00:01:56,596
But those are just the changes we hear and see.

32
00:01:56,636 --> 00:01:59,596
That’s way more going on inside.

33
00:02:00,316 --> 00:02:04,196
So, let’s talk more about our brains.

34
00:02:04,556 --> 00:02:10,076
The blue bag is a rough size and weight of a healthy baby’s brain at birth.

35
00:02:10,356 --> 00:02:14,876
The red one is a baby’s brain after the first year.

36
00:02:14,916 --> 00:02:17,436
It almost doubles in volume.

37
00:02:18,036 --> 00:02:23,836
And by my age, it's almost 90 percent the volume of an adult brain.

38
00:02:26,396 --> 00:02:29,036
Our brains develop faster in our early years

39
00:02:29,276 --> 00:02:32,756
than at any other time in our lives.

40
00:02:33,636 --> 00:02:38,676
It can create up to one million neural connections every second.

41
00:02:39,476 --> 00:02:41,036
But we need your help.

42
00:02:41,996 --> 00:02:46,396
Our healthy development depends on these top five things,

43
00:02:46,516 --> 00:02:49,996
One, connecting; two, talking;

44
00:02:50,036 --> 00:02:53,876
three, playing; four, a healthy home,

45
00:02:53,916 --> 00:02:55,836
five; community.

46
00:02:55,996 --> 00:03:00,236
All of this helps our brains and us reach our full potential.

47
00:03:00,516 --> 00:03:04,956
So what’s something you can do that can really make a difference?

48
00:03:05,356 --> 00:03:08,316
Scientists call it serve and return.

49
00:03:08,676 --> 00:03:14,356
That’s just a grown-up way of saying connect, talk and play with us.

50
00:03:14,596 --> 00:03:17,836
And here’s the really big news.

51
00:03:17,876 --> 00:03:19,956
Amarjot, Ari, you ready?

52
00:03:19,956 --> 00:03:21,436
(Audio) Amajat: Ready?

53
00:03:21,476 --> 00:03:25,036
Molly Wright: Copycat games build imagination and empathy.

54
00:03:25,516 --> 00:03:29,436
(Amarjot and Ari laughing)

55
00:03:29,716 --> 00:03:33,756
Naming games build vocabulary and attention.

56
00:03:33,796 --> 00:03:38,396
Amarjot: Daddy. Ari. Daddy. Ari.

57
00:03:38,436 --> 00:03:41,276
MW: And games like peekabo -- yep, peekaboo --

58
00:03:41,316 --> 00:03:43,596
actually build memory and trust.

59
00:03:44,156 --> 00:03:46,556
Amarjot: Peekaboo!

60
00:03:46,596 --> 00:03:51,196
MW: Each time you talk to us, play with us, make us laugh,

61
00:03:51,236 --> 00:03:56,556
it not only builds and strengthens our relationships and mental health,

62
00:03:56,596 --> 00:04:00,756
it actually teaches us some of the most important life skills,

63
00:04:00,796 --> 00:04:03,716
from making friends to taking the test,

64
00:04:03,756 --> 00:04:04,916
to getting a job,

65
00:04:04,956 --> 00:04:08,876
to one day maybe even starting a family of our own.

66
00:04:09,436 --> 00:04:12,276
Interactions early and often matter.

67
00:04:12,316 --> 00:04:13,476
Take it from me,

68
00:04:13,516 --> 00:04:17,036
the seven-year-old up here talking about brain science.

69
00:04:17,076 --> 00:04:18,516
(Laughter)

70
00:04:18,556 --> 00:04:22,916
OK, now let’s see what happens when the connection is taken away.

71
00:04:25,756 --> 00:04:28,676
So now he’s trying to get his dad’s attention again.

72
00:04:28,716 --> 00:04:32,396
He’s reaching out like, “That was fun, why have you stopped?”

73
00:04:34,596 --> 00:04:36,556
(Ari cries)

74
00:04:36,596 --> 00:04:41,436
I know it’s important for adults to use their devices sometimes,

75
00:04:41,476 --> 00:04:46,316
but kids are hardwired to seek out meaningful connections,

76
00:04:46,356 --> 00:04:50,076
not receiving them causes confusion and stress.

77
00:04:50,956 --> 00:04:54,076
OK, Amarjot, please re-engage.

78
00:04:54,556 --> 00:04:56,476
Amarjot: Ah, there’s Ari.

79
00:04:56,676 --> 00:04:59,236
(Ari laughs)

80
00:05:01,436 --> 00:05:07,356
MW: Now what if our whole childhood was like that last 30 seconds?

81
00:05:07,916 --> 00:05:10,636
How hard it would be for a child to feel calm.

82
00:05:11,676 --> 00:05:13,516
To feel safe.

83
00:05:14,156 --> 00:05:16,516
To learn to trust anyone.

84
00:05:16,556 --> 00:05:19,556
And the lifelong impact that would have.

85
00:05:20,676 --> 00:05:22,796
That makes me feel sad.

86
00:05:26,796 --> 00:05:31,756
Ari only reacted the way he did and recovered so quickly

87
00:05:31,796 --> 00:05:35,996
because the connection between him and his dad is usually so strong.

88
00:05:36,756 --> 00:05:39,836
The positive relationships with the grown-ups in our lives

89
00:05:39,876 --> 00:05:42,756
gives kids the confidence we need

14  
00:01:00,556 --> 00:01:03,756  
这些会塑造我们的童年和将来成为怎样的大人  

15  
00:01:04,556 --> 00:01:06,116  
我的信心从何而来？  

16  
00:01:06,156 --> 00:01:10,316  
是因为父母和其他人很早且频繁做到了这些事  

17  
00:01:11,156 --> 00:01:13,396  
但并非所有的孩子都这么幸运  

18  
00:01:13,436 --> 00:01:17,716  
包括我身边部分朋友、学校同学和许多其他国家的小朋友  

19  
00:01:18,196 --> 00:01:20,916  
我想尽己所能改变这一现状  

20  
00:01:22,116 --> 00:01:23,876  
多亏科学研究发现  

21  
00:01:23,876 --> 00:01:27,236  
幼儿头五年是健康发展的关键时期  

22  
00:01:27,276 --> 00:01:29,356  
尤其是大脑发育  

23  
00:01:29,396 --> 00:01:31,236  
就连在妈妈肚子里  

24  
00:01:31,276 --> 00:01:34,396  
阿瑞从尚未诞生就开始学习了  

25  
00:01:34,436 --> 00:01:36,316  
就在那位子上  

26  
00:01:36,716 --> 00:01:38,436  
刚出生时阿瑞  

27  
00:01:38,476 --> 00:01:43,236  
人小如豆整天哭哭啼啼  

28  
00:01:43,356 --> 00:01:47,596  
总说饿兮兮还要频繁换尿布  

29  
00:01:47,636 --> 00:01:49,436  
（笑声）  

30  
00:01:49,476 --> 00:01:53,236  
现在他会咯咯笑还会发出各种有趣的声音  

31  
00:01:53,276 --> 00:01:56,596  
但这些变化只是表象  

32  
00:01:56,636 --> 00:01:59,596  
脑袋里的发育远超父母想象  

33  
00:02:00,316 --> 00:02:04,196  
让我们进一步聊聊大脑发育  

34  
00:02:04,556 --> 00:02:10,076  
浅蓝色布袋的大小重量类似新生儿正常的大脑  

35  
00:02:10,356 --> 00:01:14,876  
而红色布袋显示的是周岁婴儿的大脑体积  

36  
00:02:14,916 --> 00:02:17,436  
相当于几乎翻了一倍  

37  
00:02:18,036 --> 00:02:23,836  
到我这个年龄的大脑体积已接近成人水平的90%  

38  
00:02:26,396 --> 00:02:29,036  
大脑早期发育速度是人生所有阶段里最快  

39  
00:02:29,276 --> 00:02:32,756  
一秒内能创造超过一百万个神经连接  

40  
00:02:33,636 --> 00:02:38,676  
但我们需要你们的帮助  

41  
00:02:41,996 --> 00:02:46,396  
健康发育离不开这五要素  

42  
00:02:46,516 --> 00:02:49,996  
一是连接，二是对话  

43  
00:02:50,036 --> 00:02:53,876  
三是游戏，四是健康环境  

44  
00:02:53,916 --> 00:02:55,836  
五是社群支持  

45  
00:02:55,996 --> 00:03:00,236  
这些综合因素能让我们充分开发潜能  

46  
00:03:00,516 --> 00:03:04,956  
有什么简单有效的方式能产生深远影响？  

47  
00:03:05,356 --> 00:03:08,316  
科学上称为"互动回应"  

48  
00:03:08,676 --> 00:03:14,356  
就是和我们多互动、多对话、多游戏  

49  
00:03:14,596 --> 00:03:17,836  
更重要的是这个重大发现  

50  
00:03:17,876 --> 00:03:19,956  
阿玛尔约特 阿瑞 准备好了吗？  

51  
00:03:19,956 --> 00:03:21,436  
（音频）阿玛尔约特："准备好啦？"  

52  
00:03:21,476 --> 00:03:25,036  
莫莉·莱特：模仿游戏培养想象和同理心  

53  
00:03:25,516 --> 00:03:29,436  
（阿玛尔约特和阿瑞笑声）  

54  
00:03:29,716 --> 00:03:33,756  
命名游戏能提升语言和专注力  

55  
00:03:33,796 --> 00:03:38,396  
阿玛尔约特：爸爸。阿瑞。爸爸。阿瑞。  

56  
00:03:38,436 --> 00:03:41,276  
莫莉·莱特：像藏猫猫这类游戏  

57  
00:03:41,316 --> 00:03:43,596  
能培养记忆和信任  

58  
00:03:44,156 --> 00:03:46,556  
阿玛尔约特："藏猫猫来了！"  

59  
00:03:46,596 --> 00:03:51,196  
每当你们与我们交谈嬉戏逗我们开心  

60  
00:03:51,236 --> 00:03:56,556  
不仅增进亲子关系和心理健康  

61  
00:03:56,596 --> 00:04:00,756  
更教会我们重要的生存技能  

62  
00:04:00,796 --> 00:04:03,716  
从交朋友到参加考试  

63  
00:04:03,756 --> 00:04:04,916  
找工作  

64  
00:04:04,956 --> 00:04:08,876  
甚至未来组建自己家庭的能力  

65  
00:04:09,436 --> 00:04:12,276  
高频互动至关重要  

66  
00:04:12,316 --> 00:04:13,476  
听我这个七岁女孩谈脑科学  

67  
00:04:13,516 --> 00:04:17,036  
就是最好的证明  

68  
00:04:17,076 --> 00:04:18,516  
（笑声）  

69  
00:04:18,556 --> 00:04:22,916  
现在想象下如果切断这些互动  

70  
00:04:25,756 --> 00:04:28,676  
阿瑞正试图重新引起爸爸注意  

71  
00:04:28,716 --> 00:04:32,396  
张开小手仿佛在说"刚刚多有趣，怎么停了？"  

72  
00:04:34,596 --> 00:04:36,556  
（阿瑞哭泣）  

73  
00:04:36,596 --> 00:04:41,436  
我知道成年人有时需要处理手机  

74  
00:04:41,476 --> 00:04:46,316  
但孩子天生渴望有意义的互动  

75  
00:04:46,356 --> 00:04:50,076  
缺乏回应会让孩子困惑焦虑  

76  
00:04:50,956 --> 00:04:54,076  
阿玛尔约特请重新投入互动  

77  
00:04:54,556 --> 00:04:56,476  
"噢 找到小阿瑞了！"  

78  
00:04:56,676 --> 00:04:59,236  
（阿瑞笑声）  

79  
00:05:01,436 --> 00:05:07,356  
如果整个童年都像刚才的30秒呢？  

80  
00:05:07,916 --> 00:05:10,636  
孩子要怎样才能感觉平静？  

81  
00:05:11,676 --> 00:05:13,516  
感到安全？  

82  
00:05:14,156 --> 00:05:16,516  
学会信任他人？  

83  
00:05:16,556 --> 00:05:19,556  
这些将导致终身影响  

84  
00:05:20,676 --> 00:05:22,796  
想到这里真令人心痛  

85  
00:05:26,796 --> 00:05:31,756  
阿瑞能快速恢复是因为  

86  
00:05:31,796 --> 00:05:35,996  
平时和爸爸建立了坚固的情感纽带  

87  
00:05:36,756 --> 00:05:39,836  
与养育者建立的积极关系  

88  
00:05:39,876 --> 00:05:42,756  
让我们获得探索勇气  

89  
00:05:42,796 --> 00:05:48,796  
去尝试新鲜事物 像个小科学家一样玩耍  

90  
00:05:49,916 --> 00:05:52,396  
所以请记住  

91  
00:05:52,436 --> 00:05:57,116  
孩子最重要的成长阶段是头五年  

92  
00:05:57,156 --> 00:06:00,236  
从娘胎里就开始了  

93  
00:06:00,396 --> 00:06:03,196  
有什么最影响深远的举动？  

94  
00:06:03,276 --> 00:06:06,116  
持续互动及时回应  

95  
00:06:06,556 --> 00:06:09,116  
什么时候做？  

96  
00:06:09,916 --> 00:06:11,636  
越早越好  

97  
00:06:11,756 --> 00:06:14,036  
频度要高  

98  
00:06:14,876 --> 00:06:17,756  
让我们用掌声致敬阿玛尔约特与阿瑞  

99  
00:06:17,836 --> 00:06:24,796  
（掌声）

title: Why talking to your friends can help you save money
en_source:
1
00:00:00,038 --> 00:00:02,912
Changing people's financial behavior is difficult,

2
00:00:02,912 --> 00:00:04,288
but it is possible.

3
00:00:04,663 --> 00:00:07,038
[Your Money and Your Mind with Wendy De La Rosa]

4
00:00:08,288 --> 00:00:11,913
One company that was looking to reduce energy consumption in San Diego

5
00:00:11,954 --> 00:00:13,746
tried to change people's behaviors

6
00:00:13,788 --> 00:00:16,829
by using signs with one key sentence.

7
00:00:16,829 --> 00:00:19,204
What exactly was that sentence?

8
00:00:19,246 --> 00:00:22,746
Well, it turns out that signs about protecting the environment

9
00:00:22,788 --> 00:00:24,954
or looking out for future generations

10
00:00:24,954 --> 00:00:28,121
or signs that focus on the amount of money that people will save

11
00:00:28,163 --> 00:00:31,829
were not effective in reducing consumers' energy consumption.

12
00:00:31,829 --> 00:00:34,412
Instead, the message that worked the best

13
00:00:34,412 --> 00:00:36,079
was a simple one that read,

14
00:00:36,079 --> 00:00:37,704
"The majority of your neighbors

15
00:00:37,746 --> 00:00:40,954
are undertaking energy-saving actions every day.”

16
00:00:40,996 --> 00:00:44,746
A similar message focusing on what our neighbors are doing

17
00:00:44,746 --> 00:00:48,121
was used in the UK to incentivize British taxpayers

18
00:00:48,121 --> 00:00:49,954
to pay their taxes on time.

19
00:00:49,954 --> 00:00:53,287
That simple change, pointing out what other people are doing,

20
00:00:53,329 --> 00:00:57,662
led to an increase in collections of about 29 percent.

21
00:00:57,662 --> 00:01:01,079
Psychologist Robert Cialdini, who worked on both of these studies,

22
00:01:01,121 --> 00:01:03,204
calls this phenomenon "social proof."

23
00:01:03,246 --> 00:01:06,038
He says people look to what others do

24
00:01:06,038 --> 00:01:08,454
in order to guide their own behavior.

25
00:01:08,496 --> 00:01:09,621
It's no wonder, then,

26
00:01:09,663 --> 00:01:12,329
that we base a lot of our own fiscal decisions

27
00:01:12,371 --> 00:01:13,621
on what other people do.

28
00:01:13,621 --> 00:01:16,454
And unfortunately, what we most easily observe

29
00:01:16,454 --> 00:01:18,246
are other people's spending behavior,

30
00:01:18,288 --> 00:01:19,954
not their savings behavior.

31
00:01:19,954 --> 00:01:22,621
It's easy to notice if your friend goes on vacation

32
00:01:22,621 --> 00:01:25,621
or buys a new car or a swanky pair of shoes.

33
00:01:25,663 --> 00:01:27,913
And with social media, you can even keep tabs

34
00:01:27,954 --> 00:01:30,454
on the shopping habits of the rich and the famous.

35
00:01:30,496 --> 00:01:32,288
Now, if someone wins the lottery,

36
00:01:32,329 --> 00:01:35,413
we'd expect them to spend more money -- and they do.

37
00:01:35,413 --> 00:01:36,829
But what's really interesting

38
00:01:36,829 --> 00:01:38,913
is what happens to their neighbors.

39
00:01:38,954 --> 00:01:42,621
A recent study found that close neighbors of lottery winners

40
00:01:42,621 --> 00:01:45,954
are more likely to borrow money, spend more on goods

41
00:01:45,954 --> 00:01:48,621
and eventually declare bankruptcy.

42
00:01:48,663 --> 00:01:50,913
In fact, the larger the lottery winner,

43
00:01:50,913 --> 00:01:52,829
the higher the rate of bankruptcy

44
00:01:52,871 --> 00:01:55,663
among the neighbors of the lottery winners.

45
00:01:55,704 --> 00:01:58,079
Basically, the lottery winner's behavior

46
00:01:58,121 --> 00:02:00,038
is rubbing off on their neighbors.

47
00:02:00,079 --> 00:02:02,371
We are always aware of consumer spending,

48
00:02:02,413 --> 00:02:03,871
but what we are not aware of

49
00:02:03,913 --> 00:02:06,079
are other people's savings behavior.

50
00:02:06,079 --> 00:02:07,413
So let's lift that veil.

51
00:02:07,413 --> 00:02:09,454
You can start with just a couple of friends.

52
00:02:09,496 --> 00:02:11,829
Instead of asking where they bought their new bike

53
00:02:11,871 --> 00:02:14,204
or the best time of year to travel to France,

54
00:02:14,246 --> 00:02:16,329
ask them if they paid down their mortgage

55
00:02:16,329 --> 00:02:18,079
or if they have an emergency fund

56
00:02:18,079 --> 00:02:20,121
or if they've paid off their student loan.

57
00:02:20,121 --> 00:02:22,246
Tell them about your own financial situation.

58
00:02:22,288 --> 00:02:24,121
To really make this a social affair,

59
00:02:24,163 --> 00:02:27,746
I encourage you to start celebrating paying down your debts.

60
00:02:27,788 --> 00:02:31,371
Maybe you've seen the viral video of a happy dancing woman

61
00:02:31,413 --> 00:02:35,288
who paid off more than 200,000 dollars in student debt.

62
00:02:35,288 --> 00:02:38,121
She was able to achieve this incredible milestone

63
00:02:38,121 --> 00:02:40,829
because she was bold enough to ask her colleagues

64
00:02:40,829 --> 00:02:42,121
and her industry peers

65
00:02:42,121 --> 00:02:43,371
how much they earned,

66
00:02:43,413 --> 00:02:47,079
noting the thousands of dollars that she was missing out on,

67
00:02:47,079 --> 00:02:51,829
and finding a job that would pay her her fair market rate.

68
00:02:51,829 --> 00:02:53,704
I think that video gained notoriety

69
00:02:53,746 --> 00:02:57,413
because it's not often that we get to see what people have saved

70
00:02:57,454 --> 00:02:58,871
and how they're doing it.

71
00:02:58,871 --> 00:03:00,454
But it shouldn't be so rare.

72
00:03:00,496 --> 00:03:02,329
By having check-ins with your friends,

73
00:03:02,371 --> 00:03:03,663
you can help make a trend.

74
00:03:03,704 --> 00:03:05,954
I remember when I paid off my student loan,

75
00:03:05,954 --> 00:03:08,746
I wish I would have celebrated that milestone with friends.

76
00:03:08,788 --> 00:03:11,913
But at the time, I, too, was brainwashed into thinking

77
00:03:11,954 --> 00:03:14,329
that I shouldn't talk to my friends about money,

78
00:03:14,371 --> 00:03:16,704
that it was a scary taboo subject.

79
00:03:16,704 --> 00:03:17,871
Don't be like me.

80
00:03:17,913 --> 00:03:19,621
Start the conversation today.

81
00:03:19,621 --> 00:03:22,996
Research has shown that our social bonds make us healthier.

82
00:03:23,038 --> 00:03:25,288
It's time to harness your social ties

83
00:03:25,288 --> 00:03:27,829
to boost your financial fitness, too.

84
00:03:27,871 --> 00:03:29,829
Your future self will thank you.

It's easy to notice if your friend goes on vacation

32
00:01:22,621 --> 00:01:25,621
or buys a new car or a swanky pair of shoes.

33
00:01:25,663 --> 00:01:27,913
And with social media, you can even keep tabs

34
00:01:27,954 --> 00:01:30,454
on the shopping habits of the rich and the famous.

35
00:01:30,496 --> 00:01:32,288
Now, if someone wins the lottery,

36
00:01:32,329 --> 00:01:35,413
we'd expect them to spend more money -- and they do.

37
00:01:35,413 --> 00:01:36,829
But what's really interesting

38
00:01:36,829 --> 00:01:38,913
is what happens to their neighbors.

39
00:01:38,954 --> 00:01:42,621
A recent study found that close neighbors of lottery winners

40
00:01:42,621 --> 00:01:45,954
are more likely to borrow money, spend more on goods

41
00:01:45,954 --> 00:01:48,621
and eventually declare bankruptcy.

42
00:01:48,663 --> 00:01:50,913
In fact, the larger the lottery winner,

43
00:01:50,913 --> 00:01:52,829
the higher the rate of bankruptcy

44
00:01:52,871 --> 00:01:55,663
among the neighbors of the lottery winners.

45
00:01:55,704 --> 00:01:58,079
Basically, the lottery winner's behavior

46
00:01:58,121 --> 00:02:00,038
is rubbing off on their neighbors.

47
00:02:00,079 --> 00:02:02,371
We are always aware of consumer spending,

48
00:02:02,413 --> 00:02:03,871
but what we are not aware of

49
00:02:03,913 --> 00:02:06,079
are other people's savings behavior.

50
00:02:06,079 --> 00:02:07,413
So let's lift that veil.

51
00:02:07,413 --> 00:02:09,454
You can start with just a couple of friends.

52
00:02:09,496 --> 00:02:11,829
Instead of asking where they bought their new bike

53
00:02:11,871 --> 00:02:14,204
or the best time of year to travel to France,

54
00:02:14,246 --> 00:02:16,329
ask them if they paid down their mortgage

55
00:02:16,329 --> 00:02:18,079
or if they have an emergency fund

56
00:02:18,079 --> 00:02:20,121
or if they've paid off their student loan.

57
00:02:20,121 --> 00:02:22,246
Tell them about your own financial situation.

58
00:02:22,288 --> 00:02:24,121
To really make this a social affair,

59
00:02:24,163 --> 00:02:27,746
I encourage you to start celebrating paying down your debts.

60
00:02:27,788 --> 00:02:31,371
Maybe you've seen the viral video of a happy dancing woman

61
00:02:31,413 --> 00:02:35,288
who paid off more than 200,000 dollars in student debt.

62
00:02:35,288 --> 00:02:38,121
She was able to achieve this incredible milestone

63
00:02:38,121 --> 00:02:40,829
because she was bold enough to ask her colleagues

64
00:02:40,829 --> 00:02:42,121
and her industry peers

65
00:02:42,121 --> 00:02:43,371
how much they earned,

66
00:02:43,413 --> 00:02:47,079
noting the thousands of dollars that she was missing out on,

67
00:02:47,079 --> 00:02:51,829
and finding a job that would pay her her fair market rate.

68
00:02:51,829 --> 00:02:53,704
I think that video gained notoriety

69
00:02:53,746 --> 00:02:57,413
because it's not often that we get to see what people have saved

70
00:02:57,454 --> 00:02:58,871
and how they're doing it.

71
00:02:58,871 --> 00:03:00,454
But it shouldn't be so rare.

72
00:03:00,496 --> 00:03:02,329
By having check-ins with your friends,

73
00:03:02,371 --> 00:03:03,663
you can help make a trend.

74
00:03:03,704 --> 00:03:05,954
I remember when I paid off my student loan,

75
00:03:05,954 --> 00:03:08,746
I wish I would have celebrated that milestone with friends.

76
00:03:08,788 --> 00:03:11,913
But at the time, I, too, was brainwashed into thinking

77
00:03:11,954 --> 00:03:14,329
that I shouldn't talk to my friends about money,

78
00:03:14,371 --> 00:03:16,704
that it was a scary taboo subject.

79
00:03:16,704 --> 00:03:17,871
Don't be like me.

80
00:03:17,913 --> 00:03:19,621
Start the conversation today.

81
00:03:19,621 --> 00:03:22,996
Research has shown that our social bonds make us healthier.

82
00:03:23,038 --> 00:03:25,288
It's time to harness your social ties

83
00:03:25,288 --> 00:03:27,829
to boost your financial fitness, too.

84
00:03:27,871 --> 00:03:29,829
Your future self will thank you.

1  
00:00:00,038 --> 00:00:02,912  
改变人们的理财行为很难，  

2  
00:00:02,912 --> 00:00:04,288  
但也是可以做到的。  

3  
00:00:04,663 --> 00:00:07,038  
[你的钱与你的观念---Wendy De La Rosa]  

4  
00:00:08,288 --> 00:00:11,913  
在圣地亚哥有家公司试图通过一条关键标语  

5  
00:00:11,954 --> 00:00:13,746  
改变市民的习惯，  

6  
00:00:13,788 --> 00:00:16,829  
方法就是张贴标有核心句子的提示牌。  

7  
00:00:16,829 --> 00:00:19,204  
那究竟是什么句子呢？  

8  
00:00:19,246 --> 00:00:22,746  
事实证明，强调保护环境  

9  
00:00:22,788 --> 00:00:24,954  
或为下一代着想的标语  

10  
00:00:24,954 --> 00:00:28,121  
甚至主打省钱效果的提示牌  

11  
00:00:28,163 --> 00:00:31,829  
都未能有效降低能耗。  

12  
00:00:31,829 --> 00:00:34,412  
反而是效果最好的信息是——  

13  
00:00:34,412 --> 00:00:36,079  
一句简单到极致的标语：  

14  
00:00:36,079 --> 00:00:37,704  
"你们的大多数邻居  

15  
00:00:37,746 --> 00:00:40,954  
每天都在采取节能措施。"  

16  
00:00:40,996 --> 00:00:44,746  
类似的信息在英国也被采用  

17  
00:00:44,746 --> 00:00:48,121  
激励英国纳税人按时缴税，  

18  
00:00:48,121 --> 00:00:49,954  
核心都是强调邻居行为。  

19  
00:00:49,954 --> 00:00:53,287  
这个小调整——指出别人正在做的事——  

20  
00:00:53,329 --> 00:00:57,662  
使税款回收率提升近29%。  

21  
00:00:57,662 --> 00:01:01,079  
参与两项研究的心理学家罗伯特·西奥迪尼  

22  
00:01:01,121 --> 00:01:03,204  
称此现象为"社会认同"。  

23  
00:01:03,246 --> 00:01:06,038  
人们会以他人行为为指引  

24  
00:01:06,038 --> 00:01:08,454  
来决定自己该怎么行动。  

25  
00:01:08,496 --> 00:01:09,621  
这就难怪我们做财务决策时，  

26  
00:01:09,663 --> 00:01:12,329  
总会参考他人的选择，  

27  
00:01:12,371 --> 00:01:13,621  
最直观观察到的却是消费举动——  

28  
00:01:13,621 --> 00:01:16,454  
而非储蓄行为。  

29  
00:01:16,454 --> 00:01:18,246  
若朋友去度假、买新车  

30  
00:01:18,288 --> 00:01:19,954  
或穿新潮鞋款，很容易让人注目，  

31  
00:01:19,954 --> 00:01:22,621  
而社交媒体更放大了  

32  
00:01:22,621 --> 00:01:25,621  
名流们的购物动态。  

33  
00:01:25,663 --> 00:01:27,913  
再说彩票中奖者——  

34  
00:01:27,954 --> 00:01:30,454  
我们预期他们会花大钱——当然确实如此，  

35  
00:01:30,496 --> 00:01:32,288  
但更有趣的是他们的邻居会怎样。  

36  
00:01:32,329 --> 00:01:35,413  
最新研究发现：彩票赢家的近邻  

37  
00:01:35,413 --> 00:01:36,829  
更可能增加负债  

38  
00:01:36,829 --> 00:01:38,913  
并最终宣告破产。  

39  
00:01:38,954 --> 00:01:42,621  
获奖金额越大，邻居破产率  

40  
00:01:42,621 --> 00:01:45,954  
反而攀升得越是显著。  

41  
00:01:45,954 --> 00:01:48,621  
简言之，中奖者的消费行为  

42  
00:01:48,663 --> 00:01:50,913  
正在影响他们的邻居。  

43  
00:01:50,913 --> 00:01:52,829  
我们总能观察消费动态，  

44  
00:01:52,871 --> 00:01:55,663  
却对储蓄行为知之甚少。  

45  
00:01:55,704 --> 00:01:58,079  
现在让我们揭开这层面纱。  

46  
00:01:58,121 --> 00:02:00,038  
从几位好友开始谈起吧。  

47  
00:02:00,079 --> 00:02:02,371  
下次别再问他们新单车在哪里买的，  

48  
00:02:02,413 --> 00:02:03,871  
或法国的最佳旅游时段——  

49  
00:02:03,913 --> 00:02:06,079  
试着询问他们是否在提前还房贷，  

50  
00:02:06,079 --> 00:02:07,413  
是否存了应急基金，  

51  
00:02:07,413 --> 00:02:09,454  
或有没有还清助学贷款。  

52  
00:02:09,496 --> 00:02:11,829  
也要主动分享自己的财务状况。  

53  
00:02:11,871 --> 00:02:14,204  
若想让这成为社交常态，  

54  
00:02:14,246 --> 00:02:16,329  
我建议大家将还清债务  

55  
00:02:16,329 --> 00:02:18,079  
作为值得庆祝的里程碑。  

56  
00:02:18,079 --> 00:02:20,121  
你或许见过那段爆红视频：  

57  
00:02:20,121 --> 00:02:22,246  
一位女士舞动庆祝偿还  

58  
00:02:22,288 --> 00:02:24,121  
逾20万美元学生债务。  

59  
00:02:24,163 --> 00:02:27,746  
她达成目标的关键在于敢问同事和业界同侪  

60  
00:02:27,788 --> 00:02:31,371  
各自薪资水平，  

61  
00:02:31,413 --> 00:02:35,288  
发现自己的薪酬严重不足后，  

62  
00:02:35,288 --> 00:02:38,121  
找到符合市场行情的工作。  

63  
00:02:38,121 --> 00:02:40,829  
这段视频之所以走红，  

64  
00:02:40,829 --> 00:02:42,121  
正因我们很少见到他人  

65  
00:02:42,121 --> 00:02:43,371  
的储蓄成果与方法。  

66  
00:02:43,413 --> 00:02:47,079  
这种话题不该如此罕见。  

67  
00:02:47,079 --> 00:02:51,829  
通过定期与朋友交流，  

68  
00:02:51,829 --> 00:02:53,704  
我们能打造新风潮。  

69  
00:02:53,746 --> 00:02:57,413  
我偿还助学贷款时真该这么做——  

70  
00:02:57,454 --> 00:02:58,871  
当时我深受影响，  

71  
00:02:58,871 --> 00:03:00,454  
以为不该和朋友谈钱，  

72  
00:03:00,496 --> 00:03:02,329  
误把金钱话题当禁忌。  

73  
00:03:02,371 --> 00:03:03,663  
别步我后尘。  

74  
00:03:03,704 --> 00:03:05,954  
今天就开启谈话吧。  

75  
00:03:05,954 --> 00:03:08,746  
研究证明社交联系能增进健康——  

76  
00:03:08,788 --> 00:03:11,913  
现在该善用这些关系网  

77  
00:03:11,954 --> 00:03:14,329  
提升财贸健康指数。  

78  
00:03:14,371 --> 00:03:16,704  
你的未来会感谢如今的自己。
